This is the appropriate artifact to address the senior user's concerns about catching errors caused by complex interdependencies before release. Reviewing the test plan ensures that all dependencies and their interactions are properly tested. While raising a risk is valid, the test plan is the more specific and direct solution for managing the testing of complex interdependencies.

question: You are planning your project and have captured the requirements from the project customer. You ask your team to plan the project scope, but they need to see exactly what needs to be delivered and how each part relates to each other. What will you do next?
options: A. Create a product breakdown structure with your team
B. Create the requirements traceability matrix with your project team
C. Create the risk breakdown structure with your project team
D. Create the scope management plan with your project team
explanation: A product breakdown structure is a hierarchical structure reflecting a product's components and deliverables. It provides an exhaustive hierarchical tree structure of deliverables that make up the project arranged in whole-to-parts relationships, showing exactly what needs to be delivered and how each part relates to each other. The requirements traceability matrix shows relationships to testing criteria and deliverables but does not show how parts relate to each other. The risk breakdown structure and scope management plan are not designed to show component relationships.

question: You have just finished breaking down the scope with your project team that will meet your customer's requirements. Your program manager wishes to control the project scope so that any changes go through the proper process. What will you do next?
options: A. Create the change management plan to see how changes will be managed
B. Baseline the project scope and note it in the configuration management plan
C. Create the scope management plan with a rule minimizing changes
D. Create a resource assignment matrix limiting roles and who can approve project scope changes
explanation: A baseline is the approved version of a work product or plan where actual performance is compared to the baseline to identify variances. Baselined items are noted in the configuration management plan. This establishes the formal approved scope that changes must be compared against. While a change management plan describes the process, the scope management plan describes process rules, and a resource assignment matrix defines roles, none of these establish the baseline itself that is required for scope control.

question: You have planned and begun the current sprint where the project team have committed to delivering and releasing a particular feature. You need to see how the team is tracking towards their sprint goal. What will you do next?
options: A. Review the sprint kanban board with the team
B. Review the team velocity chart
C. Review the sprint burndown chart
D. Perform a sprint review with the team
explanation: A burndown chart is a graphical representation of the work remaining in a time box such as a sprint. It compares the ideal trend with the items actually completed, showing how the team is tracking toward completing their sprint goal. A kanban board shows current sprint status but is typically just for one sprint. A velocity chart measures historical team capacity rather than current sprint progress. A sprint review demonstrates what was created during the sprint after it is complete, not how it is currently tracking.

question: Your agile project has recently been merged into a larger program and the program manager is reviewing how the project is tracking. She asks you how much has been completed in design but also how much has been completed in development, testing, and sign-off. What will you do next?
options: A. Show her the sprint kanban board
B. Take her through the team product backlog
C. Show her the team cumulative flow diagram
D. Show her the team velocity chart
explanation: A cumulative flow diagram indicates features completed over time separated at each stage such as features in the backlog, design, development, testing, and sign-off. It shows progress across multiple phases over the entire project timeline, not just a single sprint. A sprint kanban board shows only one sprint's progress. A product backlog shows work items but not their status across different phases. A velocity chart shows team capacity per sprint but does not break down completion by phase.
topic: Agile Metrics and Cumulative Flow Diagrams

question: The features developed by your agile team have added some complexity to your website, which was delivered quickly with no time to refactor the code. The code is messy and hasn't been cleaned up, and you believe the user stories are moving slower now as a result, but you can't be sure. What will you do next?
options: A. Review the cycle time chart
B. Shadow the developers to overview the work they are doing
C. View the product roadmap for proposed release dates
D. Review the points in the Sprint backlog versus the last three Sprints velocity
explanation: A cycle time chart shows the average cycle time of work items completed over time and can be displayed as a scatter diagram or bar chart. This provides visibility into how long individual user stories are taking to complete, which directly answers whether stories are moving slower. Shadowing developers (B) won't give quantitative answers needed. The product roadmap (C) won't show user story velocity. Sprint backlog points (D) show team velocity but not individual story cycle times.
topic: Agile Metrics and Artifacts - Cycle Time

question: You are working on an agile project with a full product roadmap for the next six months. Your project team is not sure what the next feature does or how they should turn it into user stories. What will you do next?
options: A. Review the scope baseline
B. Conduct story mapping with your team
C. Perform Sprint planning with your team
D. Perform a retrospective with your team
explanation: Story mapping is a visual model of all features and functionality desired for a product that breaks features down into user stories based on functionality. This directly addresses the team's need to understand what the feature does and how to decompose it into user stories. The scope baseline (A) may not be appropriate for agile projects. Sprint planning (C) requires user stories to already exist. A retrospective (D) looks at what went well or poorly, not decomposition of new features.

question: You are a program manager on a large-scale agile program where each project team has its own Scrum Master. You need to know the rate at which work is flowing through each team so you can see if there are any blockers or issues and whether they will remain on track. What will you do next?
options: A. Raise your query at the Scrum of Scrums
B. Attend the Sprint review of each team at the end of each Sprint
C. Review the schedule management plan for managing the schedule
D. Review the team velocity charts
explanation: A velocity chart tracks the rate at which deliverables are produced, validated, and accepted within a predefined interval like a Sprint. It shows what was committed versus what was completed for each team, making it the best tool to monitor the rate of work flow. Scrum of Scrums (A) is for coordination but won't provide rate metrics. Sprint reviews (B) show completed work but not rate trends. The schedule management plan (C) is a process document, not an execution metric.
topic: Agile Metrics and Artifacts - Velocity Chart

question: Your project has been executing for the past five months and due to an organizational change a new project sponsor has come on board. He asks where the project is up to and whether it is on track. He wants more information. What will you do next?
options: A. Show him the signed project charter
B. Show him the project management plan including the schedule management plan
C. Review the status report
D. Invite him to the next Sprint review
explanation: A status report provides information on the current status of the project, including progress since the last report and forecasts for cost and schedule performance. This directly answers the sponsor's question about where the project is and whether it is on track. The project charter (A) shows the original agreement but not current status. The project management plan (B) is about processes, not execution status. A Sprint review (D) shows some progress but not comprehensive tracking information across all projects.

question: Your project is releasing a number of upgrades to a commercial building over the next 12 months. Material costs have changed a lot recently. You need to budget for the materials required; however, the volume of materials may change significantly as the project evolves. What will you do next?
options: A. Create an indefinite delivery indefinite quantity (IDIQ) contract for the materials
B. Create a firm fixed price contract for the materials
C. Ensure a sole supplier for your materials
D. Ensure multiple suppliers for your materials
explanation: An indefinite delivery indefinite quantity (IDIQ) contract is the best choice when the quantity of materials may change significantly. IDIQ contracts provide for an indefinite quantity of goods or services with a stated lower and upper limit within a fixed time period, allowing flexibility as project needs evolve. A firm fixed price contract would not work because the quantity is not known and will change. While sole or multiple suppliers are considerations, they do not address the fundamental need for contract flexibility regarding quantity changes.

question: You have completed a make-or-buy analysis and you agree to hire a third-party vendor to complete part of your product. You create the statement of work and have a list of acceptable sellers that will meet the source selection criteria. The work is not complex and they understand the scope. What do you do next?
options: A. Send a request for proposal
B. Send a request for information
C. Send a request for quotation
D. Hold a bidders conference to ensure all sellers are on the same page
explanation: Since the requirements are ready, the work is not complex, the scope is understood, and you already have a list of acceptable sellers, a request for quotation (RFQ) is the most appropriate tool. A request for quotation is used when the item is straightforward and you primarily need pricing information. A request for proposal would be used for more complex items requiring detailed proposals. A request for information is used when more information is needed from sellers. A bidders conference is unnecessary since the sellers already understand the scope.

question: Your project is new and the team have just come on board. There have been multiple disagreements and the workers slow as they try to understand where they fit in the project and the team. What will you do next?
options: A. Create a project charter to kick off the project
B. Create a definition of ready to show what items need before work starts
C. Create a project management plan for all schedule, scope, and cost
D. Create a team charter to establish roles, responsibilities, and operating guidelines
explanation: A project team charter is the best solution when team members are unclear about where they fit in the project and are experiencing disagreements. The team charter records team values, agreements, and operating guidelines, establishing clear expectations regarding acceptable behavior and defining roles and responsibilities. While a project charter kicks off the project definition and a project management plan defines overall processes, neither directly addresses team dynamics and individual roles. A definition of ready applies to work items, not team structure.

question: You have created a high-level view of the features your project will deliver and prioritized them into a prioritized product backlog. The development team is ready to start work, but they do not know where to start as the information is too high level. These features are too high level and need to be broken down. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask the team to begin with the highest priority item first
B. Decompose the features into user stories to place in a Sprint
C. Hold a retrospective with the team to understand what is blocking them
D. Reprioritize the features into an order that makes sense for the developers
explanation: User stories are the appropriate tool for breaking down high-level features into work items that the development team can actually work on. A user story is a brief description of an outcome for a specific user and contains enough information at the right level for team members to work on the item. Asking the team to start with the highest priority item does not solve the decomposition problem. A retrospective is used after a Sprint to reflect on what happened, not before starting work. Reprioritizing features does not address the fundamental need to break them down into actionable items.

question: You have been assigned to a new project where the work is behind schedule and the team are not working together well. The team is relatively new and conflicts are arising. Team members are blaming each other for the work not done and some people are not showing up to work on the project at all. What will you do next?
options: A. Meet with the functional manager of that area to agree on team members' time requirements
B. Meet with your team to set a team charter and define roles and responsibilities
C. Meet with the project sponsor to gain additional funding for resources who can work together
D. Escalate the issue to the steering committee as soon as possible as a project risk
explanation: The correct answer is B. Creating a collaborative team environment through team agreements, definitions of roles and responsibilities, formal committees with specific objectives, and standing meetings regularly reviewing topics helps manage conflict and unites a new team. Escalating before managing the issue directly (A, D) is premature, and additional funding (C) does not guarantee teams will work together without conflict. The PMBOK Guide (7th Edition, pages 28-29) emphasizes establishing a collaborative team environment as the foundation for team performance.

question: You have recently completed a project for a new product and have been asked to manage it after its release to keep it relevant in today's marketplace. Half of your existing team are rolling off to other projects and the project budget is nearly depleted. What should you do?
options: A. Create a business case and a project charter for your project sponsor to approve
B. Retain as many of your current project staff to continue work on the product
C. Raise a change request for your current project and add scope and continue making changes
D. Secure funding for a stable team and use current research to form a suite of projects to improve the product
explanation: The correct answer is D. This scenario describes transitioning from project to product management. Product management involves maintaining a product throughout its life cycle with continuous improvements and feature releases. A stable team working together on the product over time becomes highly effective. Options A and C treat this as a traditional project requiring new charters and scope changes, which is inappropriate. Option B does not address the funding constraint. The PMBOK Guide (7th Edition, pages 17-19) addresses product management considerations for maintaining and improving products beyond their initial release.

question: You are delivering a critical change and several key stakeholders in your organization have added seemingly unnecessary steps for you to take with extra approvals, reports, and meetings. They are also restricting the people you need from working on your project in a larger capacity. What will you do next?
options: A. Go directly to the people you need to avoid extra back and forth with the stakeholders
B. Raise a risk in the risk register on the lack of access to sufficient resources
C. Communicate more often with the stakeholders and gain a deeper awareness of their ideas and situation
D. Show your stakeholders the resource assignment matrix and project roles and responsibilities
explanation: The correct answer is C. This situation reflects a stakeholder engagement issue. Stakeholders may restrict team members or add requirements when they are not appropriately engaged or feel uninvolved. Effective communication, knowledge sharing, and regular face-to-face meetings help win stakeholder commitment and understanding. Option A bypasses stakeholders and risks escalation. Option B merely documents the issue without addressing the root cause. Option D presents documentation but without engagement does not change behavior, as stakeholders convinced against their will maintain their original position. The PMBOK Guide (7th Edition, pages 32-33) emphasizes effectively engaging with stakeholders through communication and awareness.

question: You notice that Jane, a team member on your project, has been left out of some key meetings and others are overlooking her opinions. You also notice she has paid significantly less than her male colleagues. What will you do next?
options: A. Make steps to update her pay to the same range as the others on your project and ask her opinion and advice specifically during each meeting along with others in your team
B. Take the lower pay as a win for your project as it will help keep costs low and her colleagues do not need to know
C. Ask Jane to write down her knowledge of the industry just in case she leaves
D. No need to rock the boat; this is just how people work in this industry
explanation: The correct answer addresses integrity and stewardship by ensuring respectful engagement of project team members including fair compensation, access to opportunity, and fair treatment. Options B, C, and D avoid acting fairly and would result in poor engagement and potential team member attrition. When people discover inequality, engagement plummets, leading to knowledge loss and increased hiring costs.
topic: Integrity and Stewardship; Fair Treatment and Respectful Engagement

question: During one of your working group meetings, a stakeholder raises the concern that the product you are working on will be discontinued in the next 5 months. You are not aware of this and your project budget has already been approved until the scheduled delivery which is in 5 months. What will you do next?
options: A. Keep the project going as it is; the discontinued product is not your responsibility
B. Set a meeting with your project sponsor to share the information and provide a recommendation to terminate the project
C. Change the scope of the project to meet a different product so your project can remain relevant
D. Ask for additional funding to see if you can improve the product instead of discontinuing it
explanation: The correct answer addresses delivering value and focusing on the primary motivator for a project. If the project is no longer aligned with the business need or unlikely to provide value, the organization should terminate the effort. This requires meeting with the sponsor to share the critical information and recommend appropriate action. Continuing the project, changing scope mid-stream, or seeking additional funding does not address the fundamental misalignment with business value.
topic: Delivering Value; Project Termination; Stakeholder Communication

question: You have collected the requirements for your project and are creating the scope statement and work breakdown structure. During this process, you notice some functionality in your database system that will help a different project that you are aware of in your project management office. What will you do next?
options: A. Place the feature in the other project's backlog and ask them to prioritize it as soon as possible
B. Raise the functionality in your risk register as a threat with the response to mitigate
C. Add the scope to your project and complete it as soon as possible
D. Raise the functionality in your risk register as an opportunity with the response to exploit and meet with the other project team and sponsor to discuss
explanation: The correct answer applies systems thinking by taking a holistic view of how project parts interact with each other and external systems. Finding a previously unknown opportunity requires making it known and exploiting it through stakeholder discussion. Option A assumes authority to add to another project's backlog without discussion. Option B misclassifies this as a threat rather than an opportunity. Option C adds out-of-scope work that your team may not be experts in. Meeting with the other project team and sponsor ensures proper integration and resource allocation.
topic: Systems Thinking; Opportunity Management; Stakeholder Engagement

question: You are working on a high-profile project and several high-ranking senior executives want to take credit for this initiative while limiting the impact to their own resources. This has led to several conflicts and an unclear scope statement, resulting in a risk of project failure. What will you do next?
options: A. Create a scope statement yourself to avoid further conflict
B. Facilitate discussion with the executives as a neutral third party focusing on agreed project goals
C. Allow the executives to define the scope based on their preferred allocation of resources
D. Escalate the conflict to senior leadership without attempting resolution
explanation: The correct answer uses facilitation techniques to align stakeholders around agreed project goals, which is a key project manager responsibility. Acting as a neutral third party helps resolve conflicts and build consensus on scope. Option A risks stakeholder rejection and customer non-acceptance. Option C perpetuates the resource-hoarding behavior causing the conflict. Option D escalates prematurely without attempting to resolve through proper facilitation and stakeholder engagement.
topic: Stakeholder Engagement; Conflict Resolution; Facilitation; Scope Definition

question: You are managing a project where senior managers are suffering from conflict over priorities. What leadership approach will be most effective in resolving this situation?
options: A. Provide detailed recommendations on how to resolve the conflict
B. Use neutral facilitation to help senior managers resolve the problem themselves
C. Escalate the issue to the project steering committee
D. Limit project resources to the executives until they can agree on a way forward
explanation: Neutral facilitation helps more than detailed recommendations because when leaders generate ideas themselves and reach consensus, they become engaged with those ideas and are more likely to support the solution. Providing recommendations without their input lacks their buy-in. Escalation should only occur after exhausting management options. Limiting resources continues to waste project funds.

question: You are managing a project where the majority of the product has been planned upfront, but an external system is needed from a vendor in partial pieces where the requirements are unclear. Half of your stakeholders want to use a waterfall approach and the other half are advocating for an agile approach. What will you do next?
options: A. Use an agile methodology because the fixed scope is low risk and you can capture changes using agile
B. Use a waterfall methodology
C. Proceed with a hybrid method to start with pre-planned scope but agile ceremonies that promote feedback
D. Use a combination of waterfall and agile to capture all requirements and keep stakeholders happy
explanation: A hybrid approach is the most appropriate tailoring strategy when different parts of the project require different methodologies. The pre-planned portion can use waterfall while the unclear external system requirements benefit from agile ceremonies and feedback loops. This combines both stakeholder needs and project contexts. While option D mentions both approaches, option C uses the specific terminology of hybrid methodology and includes the tailoring principle of adapting to unique project contexts.

question: You are working on a project where senior users have been testing the product as parts are released. They have found some missing requirements and defects. Their manager is worried that the project won't deliver a product that is fit for purpose without defects and within the needed timeframe. What will you do next?
options: A. Perform some of the testing yourself to ensure a quality product
B. Help every team member focus on quality from correct acceptance criteria to a developed and tested product
C. Limit testing until the very end of the project so you can do it all in one go when the product is completely ready
D. Increase testing on your project and add more testing resources so no defect goes unnoticed
explanation: In an agile project, quality is everyone's responsibility, not just the project manager's or a dedicated testing team's. By focusing the entire team on quality through proper acceptance criteria and building quality into processes, you address the root cause rather than just adding resources. Testing should occur at all levels throughout the project (unit testing, system testing, user acceptance testing, regression testing), not just at the end. The project manager acts as a conductor orchestrating the team, not performing individual technical tasks.

question: You are working through the risk assessment with your team who are having trouble coming up with ideas for risks. You decide to give them some guidance on types of complexity that might affect your new product. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask them to brainstorm qualitative and quantitative risks
B. Ask them to perform the five whys and Ishikawa analysis to find the root cause of a problem
C. Ask them to brainstorm around possible unwanted human behavior, system behavior, ambiguity, and technical innovation impacts
D. Ask them to perform a retrospective to discover what isn't working well and what still puzzles us
explanation: Complexity is often the result of human behavior, system interactions, technical innovation, uncertainty, and ambiguity (VUCA framework). By guiding the team to brainstorm risks across these types of complexity, you help them identify risks more comprehensively. This addresses the underlying sources of complexity that can introduce risks affecting value, scope, stakeholders, and project success. The five whys and Ishikawa are root cause analysis tools used after a problem is identified, not for generating risks. Retrospectives are review tools, not risk identification techniques.

question: You are working on a project where you discover a certain negative risk with a potential impact of $2 million. You meet with your project stakeholders who determine that this is within their risk appetite provided that you meet a risk threshold of 10%. What will you do next?
options: A. Note the acceptable risk level as a threat of $200,000, which is 10%
B. Note the acceptable risk level as a threat of $1.8 million to $2.2 million, which is a 10% variance
C. Note the impact as outside the acceptable risk level of $2 million
D. Note the impact as an opportunity of $200,000, which is 10%
explanation: Risk appetite describes the level of uncertainty or risk stakeholders are willing to accept. Risk threshold is the measure of acceptable variation around an objective that reflects the risk appetite. In this scenario, a 10% variation around $2 million is $1.8 million to $2.2 million, which matches the stakeholders' acceptable risk parameters. The other options either misrepresent the calculation or ignore stakeholder acceptance.

question: A risk has been raised in your project around insufficient building materials. A risk responder assigned to the functional manager suggests using a brand new material which has not been fully tested before in a building project. Half of your stakeholder group are happy with this risk response but the other half are not and refuse to sign off on it. What will you do next?
options: A. Suggest a quantitative risk approach to give a more accurate financial impact
B. Suggest they proceed with the proposed risk response and ensure it is followed through
C. Suggest a different risk owner as an executive may have more power to respond to the risk
D. Suggest a different risk response as they should be timely, cost-effective, realistic, and agreed to by relevant stakeholders
explanation: Risk responses should be appropriate and timely to the significance of the risk, cost-effective, realistic within the project context, agreed to by relevant stakeholders, and owned by a responsible person. Since 50% of stakeholders are disagreeing with the proposed response, it does not meet the criterion of being agreed to by relevant stakeholders. The best approach is to suggest a different risk response that addresses these concerns. Forcing the response through or changing the risk owner does not resolve stakeholder disagreement.

question: Your project is starting to go off track due to a large amount of uncertainty in the requirements and resources. You managed to reign in these issues at a significant cost. However, your project management office would like you to brainstorm ways to improve with your team so that it does not happen again. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask your team to increase the project contingency reserve to allow for these impacts
B. Ask your team to change the project model methodology from waterfall to agile
C. Ask your team to use short feedback loops, continuous improvement, and transparent planning
D. Ask your team to check their work twice before sending it to the next part of the process
explanation: Project teams need to embrace adaptability and resiliency with methods such as using short feedback loops, continuous learning and improvement, regular inspection, transparent planning, and diverse project teams. These agile practices enable teams to identify and address uncertainty and requirements issues earlier in the process. While changing methodology or increasing contingency reserves may help, short feedback loops and transparent planning directly address the root causes of the uncertainty and allow for continuous adaptation. Merely checking work twice does not address the underlying requirement for adaptability.

question: As your project progresses, many of the business stakeholders are starting to voice their concerns, question some of the changes, and even miss key meetings while trying to sabotage your efforts. What will you do next?
options: A. Communicate the vision and benefits for the change clearly along with the impact to their work
B. Create an impact over influence chart to determine who should be engaged appropriately
C. Escalate the matter to your project sponsor and arrange for different stakeholders
D. Map the current state and the proposed future state of the change for the stakeholders
explanation: Stakeholder resistance is best addressed through clear communication of vision, benefits, and impact. This two-way communication approach allows you to listen to stakeholder concerns while articulating the value of proposed changes. While tools like impact-influence charts and state mapping are useful planning techniques, they do not directly address the immediate engagement and communication gaps. Escalating to replace stakeholders is typically not feasible or advisable, as it does not resolve the underlying concerns and may exacerbate resistance.

question: You are analyzing your stakeholders through an impact over influence chart and have found more than 100 different groups of people and over 3,000 impacted stakeholders. Other parts of your project are starting to fall behind as you try to keep up with a large amount of people. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask your project team to engage different groups of stakeholders so everyone is covered
B. Clearly prioritize the stakeholders
C. Engage and communicate with the highly impacted and influential groups early and often
D. Create a SharePoint page for the changes that any stakeholder can gather information from anytime
explanation: When faced with an overwhelming number of stakeholders (over 100 groups and 3,000 impacted stakeholders), prioritization is the key strategy. While engagement, communication, and information sharing are all important components of stakeholder management, the root issue is that resources are stretched thin trying to manage everyone equally. Prioritization using impact and influence allows the project manager to focus efforts on the stakeholders who matter most, freeing up resources and preventing project delays. Stakeholder engagement includes identifying, understanding, analyzing, prioritizing, engaging, and monitoring stakeholders. Impact and influence are two primary ways to prioritize stakeholders.

question: You are managing a project which communicates to the customer using mostly pull communication so far. When monitoring the engagement of your stakeholders, you notice that they are not clear on the impacts or benefits of the project. You decide to add push communication to your communication plan. What will you do next?
options: A. Create a web page using SharePoint with all the project information that your stakeholders can see
B. Push more funding for your project and communicate this to the project sponsor
C. Push your stakeholders to engage in more two-way communication
D. Send a weekly email to stakeholders with a project update including the impacts and benefits
explanation: Push communication involves sending information directly to stakeholders using memos, emails, status reports, voicemail, and similar methods. Pull communication, by contrast, is information that stakeholders can access at their own discretion from sources like bulletin boards, web pages, or whiteboards. Since stakeholders are unclear on project impacts and benefits and pull communication alone has not been effective, adding push communication through weekly emails with this specific information addresses the gap. This is an active, direct method of ensuring stakeholders receive critical project information rather than relying on them to seek it out.

question: You have taken over a project with a vendor where scope and requirements have been missed. You notice that miscommunication and mixed messages are common. You set a meeting with your team and the vendor team. The meeting is extremely important to get right. What will you do next?
options: A. Speak slowly and clearly with the vendor team at a louder volume if necessary
B. Confirm they hear your message, determine if they agree, and identify nuanced or unintended messages that they may have received
C. Only communicate with the vendor in writing so you have a trail of proof if anything goes wrong in the future
D. Prepare a claim for the mishandled scope of your project through the procurement team
explanation: Effective communication requires feedback loops and verification. Simply speaking louder does not improve comprehension. Writing-only communication removes critical non-verbal elements (body language, tonality, visual cues) that account for a significant portion of communication meaning, making misinterpretation more likely. Preparing a claim prematurely bypasses the opportunity to resolve the issue through communication. The correct approach is to establish a two-way dialogue where you confirm the vendor team has heard your message, determine if they actually agree, and identify any nuanced or unintended messages they may have received. This active listening and feedback approach prevents further miscommunication and addresses the root cause of the scope and requirements gaps.

question: You are working on a project where issues are often raised and result in multiple rounds of feedback. You are not sure who or what could be the cause of these issues. What will you do next?
options: A. Perform root cause analysis with your project team on the problem to brainstorm ideas
B. Review the issue log, the risk register, and the change log for the most frequent requesters, then update your stakeholder engagement approach for those people
C. Raise a risk in the risk register for the project delay and review responses with your team
D. Ensure your project budget has enough contingency reserves to meet the multiple reviews and delays
explanation: The correct answer addresses the root cause by identifying who is requesting changes (from the issue log, risk register, and change log) and then improving engagement with those stakeholders. While root cause analysis is a reasonable approach, the best answer goes further by examining patterns of who is making requests and updating the stakeholder engagement strategy accordingly. This addresses both the 'who' and ensures better two-way communication to potentially prevent future issues.

question: You have been working on a project where the manager leads in a dictatorial style. Over time, the team have stopped making suggestions, sick days and mental health days have become more frequent, and the pace of work has slowed. The manager comes to you for help and you suggest a servant leadership style instead. What will they do next?
options: A. Serve the team by ensuring their every need is met
B. Focus on obstacle removal, encouragement, and development opportunities
C. Ensure the team are serving other team members and helping each other succeed
D. Serve the team through new ways to do the work and engage them through additional variety
explanation: Servant leadership specifically involves removing blockers that prevent the team from doing their work, shielding them from external distractions, encouraging their growth, and creating development opportunities. While ensuring every need is met sounds caring, servant leaders empower teams by removing obstacles and enabling growth rather than meeting every need. The other options reference good practices but do not capture the core essence of servant leadership as defined in the PMBOK Guide.

question: You have been allocated to a new project team with members from different parts of the functional business area. Very few of them have worked on a project before. There is a lot of miscommunication and rework occurring amongst the team, which is starting to cause delays. What will you do next?
options: A. Set a daily standup with your team to ensure they update what they completed
B. Review the resource assignment matrix with your team to ensure they are doing their job
C. Help the team set their vision and objectives, roles and responsibilities through a team charter
D. Send your team members on training in their roles to increase their capability
explanation: The best answer addresses the root cause of miscommunication and rework by establishing clarity on vision, objectives, roles, and responsibilities through team development activities like a team charter. This creates a shared understanding of how the team will work together and how they contribute to the project. While the other options are valid practices, they do not establish the foundational team clarity needed when members lack project experience and are experiencing miscommunication.

question: You have collected the requirements for your project, matched them to project scope, and broken them down into smaller parts and the activities required. You notice that the manager of the marketing department has added extra scope that is unrelated to your current project. What will you do next?
options: A. Start an open dialogue with the functional manager
B. Raise the incorrect scope items and their impact and start an open dialogue
C. Override the marketing manager's decision on scope as you are the senior project manager
D. Raise a change request for the additional scope and let the Change Control Board decide
explanation: The best answer combines two actions: raising awareness of the incorrect scope items and their impact while also initiating dialogue. This approach addresses the issue directly by documenting what is out of scope and why, while maintaining open communication with the functional manager. Starting dialogue alone (option A) without first establishing the scope mismatch is less effective. Overriding the manager is inappropriate given their authority, and jumping to the change control process (option D) skips the necessary discussion to determine if a change is even warranted.

question: Your project team are protected by a union agreement. You notice that they don't know how to use the product you're improving, they're slow to respond, and regularly complain about the work. Your project sponsor has had enough and threatens to shut down the project if improvements can't be made. What will you do next?
options: A. Set up a new project with different team members and transfer the work to that team
B. Set a clear project purpose and vision and pair your team with a customer
C. Tell your project team that they will lose their jobs
D. Create a project Gantt chart schedule and burndown chart so the work is transparent to all
explanation: Setting a clear vision and purpose, and pairing the team with a customer, increases intrinsic motivation by showing team members how they are making a positive difference. Transferring to another team risks the same results and requires going through team development stages again. Threatening job loss embeds negative behavior further rather than changing it. While transparency is good, a Gantt chart and burndown chart don't directly address the core engagement and motivation issues.

question: During a key scope meeting, an argument arises. The business manager accuses the technology manager of not delivering to their needs on past projects, and the technology manager accuses the business manager of changing requirements that impact the delivery schedule. The meeting is at risk with the stakeholders being closed off to any further discussion. What will you do next?
options: A. Take the side of the technology manager as scope that impacts delivery should be controlled
B. Take the side of the business manager as we should always deliver to the customer specification
C. Facilitate to keep the communication respectful, focus on the issue in the present, and search for alternatives together
D. Ensure they both agree to the Change Control process to avoid disagreements in the future
explanation: Facilitating respectful communication, focusing on the issue (not the person) and the present (not the past), and searching for alternatives together leads to better decision-making and stronger solutions. Taking sides ignores that both perspectives have merit. While agreeing to a Change Control process is important, problem-solving and addressing the current issue must happen first. This approach aligns with open and respectful communication practices for conflict management.

question: You are working on a project where the project team is completely distributed around the country in multiple sites, including working from home. Some of the project team members have not been able to contribute to the project, and there are concerns from the program that the project will fall behind. What will you do next?
options: A. Raise a request to the program to collocate all your project team members as soon as possible
B. Proceed with daily stand-ups and working group meetings with any team members in your area
C. Set team leaders for each area around the country to report back to you as the centralized project leader
D. Use technology to maintain ongoing contact with audio and video for meetings, a team website to keep all project information available
explanation: Using technology for audio, video, and messaging, combined with a centralized project website for transparency and information availability, enables effective collaboration across distributed teams without requiring collocation. Collocating is often not feasible. Meetings only with team members in your area excludes distributed members. Setting up separate area leaders creates silos and reduces collaboration. Technology-enabled transparency and ongoing contact keeps all team members engaged and informed.
topic: Distributed Project Teams, Remote Team Management, Communication

question: You are initiating a project for a sales team where the process will change significantly, even though the outcome of a completed sale will remain the same. The functional manager is concerned about the change and any disruption that it might bring. What will you do next?
options: A. Kick off the project with multiple deliveries to deliver small parts and reduce the change impact
B. Kick off the project with a single delivery at the end of the project when the process change is complete
C. Kick off the project with continuous delivery in mind to ensure fast delivery to the business
D. Kick off the project using periodic deliveries as delivering value as it's ready
explanation: For process re-engineering projects, a single delivery approach is preferred because you are changing one complete process all at once. A process re-engineering project may not have any deliveries until near the end of the project when the new process is rolled out and ready. This is covered in the PMBOK Guide, 7th Edition, page 34, under Delivery Cadence.

question: You are working on a project where the requirements are clear but the solution ideas are uncertain. The product owner and technical experts cannot be sure which ideas will work in practice and which ones will not. The organization cannot afford to get this project wrong because they have previously made mistakes and customers are starting to leave. What will you do next?
options: A. Use an incremental approach so we deliver increments of value progressively
B. Use an iterative approach so we iterate towards success while delivering in one release
C. Use a predictive development approach that will plan everything up front and then deliver in one go
D. Use a hybrid approach that is part agile and part waterfall
explanation: An iterative approach is used when you need to capture ideas that might change while still delivering in one release. Since the organization cannot afford customer-facing incremental releases but needs feedback to validate which solutions work, an iterative approach allows you to refine ideas through feedback loops while maintaining a single final release. This is covered in the PMBOK Guide, 7th Edition, page 37, under Development Approaches.

question: You are working on a new product for your organization where the requirements are complex and are likely to change a few times as the project progresses. The organization also needs usable parts of the product to be released as fast as possible to capture market share away from competitors. What will you do next?
options: A. Use an iterative approach with one big bang release but with feedback
B. Use a predictive approach with planned releases
C. Use an adaptive or agile approach that delivers increments plus iterations with feedback
D. Use a hybrid approach that is a combination of agile and waterfall
explanation: Adaptive approaches are useful when requirements are subject to uncertainty and change and a clear vision is established at the start of the project. Initial known requirements are refined or replaced in accordance with user feedback as you progress and unexpected events occur. This combines incremental delivery of usable product parts with iterative refinement based on feedback. This is covered in the PMBOK Guide, 7th Edition, page 35, under Adaptive Approach.

question: A new project has been kicked off in your organization due to a recent government inquiry into your industry which found multiple processes with significant regulatory oversight that need to change. The inquiry has given you a due date of 10 months before you start incurring penalties. What will you do next?
options: A. Proceed with an agile development approach to deliver change as quickly as possible
B. Proceed with a hybrid development approach to respond to changes while planning up front
C. Proceed with a predictive development approach due to the required process documentation and demonstration needs
D. Proceed with an iterative methodology to improve using feedback
explanation: Environments that have significant regulatory oversight may need to use a predictive approach due to the required process documentation and demonstration needs. When dealing with government regulations and compliance requirements, planning everything up front and demonstrating adherence to regulatory requirements is more important than rapid iteration. This is covered in the PMBOK Guide, 7th Edition, page 40, under Considerations for Selecting a Development Approach.
topic: Development Approach Selection for Regulated Environments

question: Your team has been working at a sustainable pace, but your organization is moving to an agile way of work. The team has hesitations about this transition. What should you do?
options: A. Add extra resources to your team to deliver faster and avoid executives getting involved
B. Proceed with a predictive development approach and deal with the consequences later
C. Ask your team to work with agile velocity and iterations despite their hesitations
D. Suggest the team uses flow-based scheduling with a Kanban board for visual work management
explanation: Flow-based scheduling with Kanban boards is a proven agile approach that respects team capacity and maintains focus through work-in-progress limits, minimizing context switching and multitasking. This addresses the transition to agile while supporting sustainable pace. Adding resources may complicate things, forcing the team against their will reduces engagement, and avoiding the organizational mandate is not viable. Kanban is one of the major agile approaches alongside Scrum and Lean, optimizing deliveries based on resource capacity and minimizing waste.

question: Your team has never worked on projects before and doesn't know about waterfall, agile, or different development models. Your project may have requirements that are certain or uncertain with varying levels of risk, and you are unsure which approach to use as you begin this new project. What will you do next?
options: A. Use waterfall and plan everything up front so the team has more stability
B. Use general phase definitions such as feasibility, design, build, test, deploy, and close
C. Use agile to ensure you can respond to change while your team's capability increases
D. Don't use any project approach; it doesn't matter as long as the work is getting done
explanation: All projects—whether waterfall or agile—go through the same fundamental phases: feasibility, design, build, test, deploy, and close. In waterfall these are done sequentially once; in agile they are done multiple times for each feature or increment. This approach provides a universally applicable starting point regardless of uncertainty or team experience. It allows flexibility in how these phases are implemented while ensuring a structured framework is in place.
topic: Project Life Cycles and Phase Definitions

question: You are a project manager in an organization with a directive project management office and are currently between projects. The PMO asks you to prepare for an upcoming project in the accounting area by meeting with the functional manager. You have no team, no scope, no requirements, and no timeline yet. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask the functional manager for their wish list of improvements so you can take these to your PMO
B. Create a project management plan so the project scope, budget, and schedule are more clear
C. Create a small backlog of work and start your first sprint, then progressively update the backlog as the project progresses
D. Progressively elaborate the vision and develop a vision statement and project charter to kick off your project
explanation: Before initiating any project, you must first establish a vision statement, project charter, or business case to identify a coordinated path forward and understand why the project exists. This establishes direction and enables all subsequent planning activities. Creating a full project management plan is premature without a charter; starting sprints without clarity is not appropriate; and gathering a wish list alone is insufficient without first defining the strategic direction and coordinated approach.

question: You are working on a project where part of the project delivers a high-value, high-risk component and another part delivers routine changes to the system. Your project sponsor wants to gain business value quickly, but your project team wants to start with the work they are familiar with first. What will you do next?
options: A. Prioritize the higher-risk items at the end of the project
B. Prioritize the high-risk items at the start of the project
C. Prioritize the routine work first since the team is familiar with it
D. Split the work equally between high-risk and routine components
explanation: High-risk items should be prioritized at the start of the project to remove risk early. As a project progresses, risk exposure increases—if high-risk items remain unresolved until the end, the risk of project failure or downstream impacts becomes significantly higher. Addressing high-risk components first reduces overall project risk and enables course correction if issues arise, ultimately protecting the high business value component.

question: You are working on an agile project where the functional manager you are delivering to only has a high level idea of the system requirements that they will need. The functional manager suggests a work breakdown structure; however, your team feels that is more for a predictive or waterfall delivery approach. What will you do next?
options: A. Create and use a work breakdown structure anyway as it is just a tool
B. Use rolling wave planning and keep the work at a high level until you are ready to work on it, then you can plan in detail
C. Note the high level themes or epics, decompose them into features and again into user stories
D. Use the functional area for more detailed requirements before the project begins
explanation: In agile projects, decomposition is still applied but in an agile-centric way. Breaking down work from epics into features to user stories is a form of decomposition that fits the incremental delivery approach. While rolling wave planning is also valid, decomposing into user stories is more specifically aligned with agile methodology and represents a work breakdown structure in agile terms. The question references page 54 under delivery in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You are at the beginning of planning a new project and have researched and gathered the requirements from the area, broken them down into deliverables and activities, which you believe could take 26 weeks to deliver. The project sponsor is asking you for an estimate on how long the project will take. What will you do next?
options: A. Tell the project sponsor 26 weeks
B. Tell the project sponsor you will use an iterative approach where value is delivered until the project stops
C. Tell the project sponsor approximately 24 to 28 weeks
D. Tell the project sponsor approximately 20 to 45 weeks
explanation: At the beginning of a project when there is limited information, estimates should have a broad range. The appropriate range at project initiation is approximately minus 25% to plus 75%, which corresponds to the 20 to 45 weeks range. As the project progresses and the team gains more knowledge, the range narrows. Early estimates without significant detail warrant wider ranges to account for uncertainty. This concept is referenced on page 55 under estimating and range in the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition.

question: You are working on a business case where you know that the industry is undergoing a significant change at the moment and there could be many different impacts to your project benefits before it is delivered completely. Your project sponsor asks you for the best estimate of benefits that you can give under the circumstances. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask your team for a probabilistic estimate
B. Ask your team for a deterministic estimate
C. Ask your team for an absolute estimate
D. Ask your team for a relative estimate
explanation: Probabilistic estimates include a range of estimates along with associated probabilities, which is appropriate when there is uncertainty and significant environmental change. A deterministic or absolute estimate would be a specific fixed number, which is not suitable when the environment is changing and impacts are uncertain. Relative estimates are used for comparing items to each other (common in agile), not for estimating absolute benefit values. In conditions of uncertainty with multiple potential impacts, a probabilistic approach with confidence levels or probability distributions is the best approach.

question: You have noted nine epics for delivery as part of your most recent project. Your team have broken down these epics into user stories and would like to see the effort involved for these deliverables. You ask your team to find the smallest user story, size it with a one, and then determine the size of the other cards based on how they compare to the original card. What approach are you taking?
options: A. Relative estimating
B. Deterministic estimating
C. Absolute estimating
D. Probabilistic estimating
explanation: This approach describes relative estimating, where you establish a baseline (the smallest user story sized as one) and then estimate all other items in comparison to that baseline. This is commonly used in agile environments and forms the basis for story point estimation. Deterministic and absolute estimating produce specific fixed numbers. Probabilistic estimating involves probability ranges and confidence levels, not comparative sizing.

question: You are working on a project where a competitor is also working on a similar feature and being first to the market will have a significant impact on this year's profit. Your product owner would like to measure how quickly story cards are being completed once they start being developed. What will you do next?
options: A. Check the Gantt chart
B. Check the schedule network and precedent diagram
C. Measure the cycle time
D. Measure the lead time
explanation: Cycle time is the total elapsed time it takes one unit to get through a process from when a developer starts picking it up until they complete their work. This directly measures how quickly story cards are being completed once development begins. Lead time measures from when the customer makes an order or requirement is noted until delivery, which is broader than what is being asked. Gantt charts and network diagrams are schedule tools not specifically designed to measure card completion speed in a flow-based process.
topic: Flow-Based Estimating (Lead Time and Cycle Time)

question: You are working on a project that has recently moved to a more agile way of work. The team have not completely taken on the scrum approach however and are comfortable working with a more flow-based approach, moving user stories across a kanban board. You are wanting to get an estimate on how much work can be completed in the next three months based on the team's existing process progress. What will you do next?
options: A. Multiply the velocity by the number of remaining user stories
B. Multiply the cycle time by the throughput
C. Use story points to forecast delivery
D. Calculate sprint burn-down rate
explanation: In a flow-based (kanban) approach, you multiply the cycle time (average time for each user story to be completed) by the throughput (how many user stories are being completed per unit time, such as per week or month) to forecast how much work can be completed in three months. Multiplying velocity by remaining user stories is a scrum-specific approach. Story points and sprint burn-down rates are also scrum-specific metrics, not appropriate for a flow-based kanban process.

question: Your product owner wants to bring the next feature forward so that it is done in parallel to the current feature. You make the necessary adjustments to the product backlog and decide to adjust the product roadmap which you have visualized as a Gantt chart. What will you do next to make this happen?
options: A. Use a schedule lag
B. Use a schedule lead
C. Change the feature to finish-to-start dependency
D. Change the feature to start-to-start dependency
explanation: Schedule lead is used to bring an item forward in time. Using the precedence diagramming method with a finish-to-start relationship, you can apply a lead so the second item can start before the first item finishes, allowing parallel work while maintaining delivery order. Schedule lag moves items backward, not forward. While finish-to-start is the current dependency, a lead applied to it achieves the goal, making schedule lead the best answer.

question: You are working on a project where you have been completing the deliverables in sequence. The project work is starting to fall behind. The project sponsor asks you to speed up the work and reduce the time for the project to be delivered but also makes it very clear that there is no more money to use when speeding up the work. What will you do next?
options: A. Use schedule crashing to add resources
B. Use schedule fast tracking to perform activities in parallel
C. Use resource leveling to balance the schedule
D. Negotiate with the sponsor for additional budget
explanation: Fast tracking is the appropriate schedule compression technique when budget constraints exist. It involves performing activities that are normally done in sequence in parallel instead. Schedule crashing requires additional resources and budget, which are not available. Resource leveling actually makes schedules longer by sequencing parallel work. Fast tracking is the only option that compresses the schedule without requiring additional money.
topic: Schedule Compression - Fast Tracking vs Crashing

question: After reviewing your project schedule, you notice you are behind by 3 weeks and the schedule needs to be compressed. Your project does not have enough budget to crash the schedule. You check with the lead for each area and review the schedule as to whether they can fast track their deliverables. Which dependencies should you focus on when fast tracking?
options: A. Discretionary dependencies
B. Mandatory dependencies
C. External dependencies
D. Industry-regulated dependencies
explanation: Discretionary dependencies are based on project preferences and are the most easily modifiable. They are not set in stone and can be adjusted for fast tracking. Mandatory dependencies are fixed requirements and cannot be modified. External dependencies are outside project control and difficult to change. Industry-regulated dependencies have strict regulatory requirements and cannot be modified. When compressing schedules through fast tracking, focus on discretionary dependencies as they offer the most flexibility.

question: You are transitioning your project from waterfall to Agile. Your key customer asks you for a project schedule. The project team believes that using a project schedule is from the old waterfall way of working and they shouldn't have to create one. What will you do next?
options: A. Create a project Gantt chart to visualize the activities within the project
B. Determine the project's planned versus actual value instead of working with a schedule directly
C. Advise the customer that we don't need to plan ahead in an adaptive way of work
D. Ask your team to develop a high-level release plan like a product road map showing the features to be included in each release
explanation: In adaptive/Agile approaches, a high-level release plan (product road map) shows basic features and functionality to be included in each release using incremental planning. This addresses the customer's need for scheduling while aligning with Agile principles. While Gantt charts can work, a product road map is more Agile-centric. Planned versus actual value focuses on variance analysis rather than scheduling. Advising against planning ahead is incorrect—product road maps are essential for showing upcoming features and ensuring value delivery.

question: You are working in a project team that has recently moved to an Agile way of work. Your business analysts want to gather all requirements, match solutions to scope, and create a work breakdown structure for the whole deliverable. Your product owner says this will take too long and is not Agile enough. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask your team to review the scope management plan for the proper process
B. Break the deliverable down into smaller pieces so you can deliver those faster
C. Ask your team to time box work on the highest priority items in the backlog, update the estimates, and then reprioritize them as necessary
D. Review the project charter for the high-level scope and then use this to begin delivery quickly
explanation: Time boxing work on highest-priority backlog items and reprioritizing as necessary is the most Agile-centric approach. It incorporates key Agile practices: prioritization, time boxing, estimate updates, and continuous reprioritization. Breaking deliverables into smaller pieces (B) may not create usable increments. Reviewing the scope management plan (A) and project charter (D) do not address the Agile concern about spending too much time upfront on complete analysis. Adaptive approaches emphasize time boxes and prioritized backlogs where teams self-manage their work.

question: While reviewing current project progress with your project sponsor, they notice that the system solution is missing a critical piece that will impact the benefit of the project. The project sponsor approves this scope addition. Your project has a planned value of $520,000 and an actual cost of $535,000. What will you do next to add this critical piece of scope?
options: A. Work with the PMO to unlock the project management reserves for the extra work
B. Ask the product owner to reprioritize the backlog and see if the new work will fit
C. Perform the work within your normal budget as your project is on track
D. Raise a change request for the changes and gain approval from the Change Control Board
explanation: Management reserves are specifically set aside for unexpected activities related to in-scope work. Since the project sponsor has already approved this scope as necessary for benefit realization, it qualifies as in-scope work that should be funded through management reserves, which may be managed by the PMO at program or portfolio levels. Option B assumes an Agile project and would displace other work. Option C is incorrect because the project is over budget ($535,000 actual vs. $520,000 planned). Option D is unnecessary since the project sponsor (typically part of the Change Control Board) has already approved it.

question: You are in the planning stages of an Agile project where functional managers and senior users have provided high-level requirements and scope. You have been asked to put together a project team that will be able to deliver quickly. What will you do next?
options: A. Select project team members from each city to ensure diversity of knowledge within the team
B. Put together a resource management plan outlining the resources
C. Focus on team collocation and transparency in the work environment to enable fast delivery
D. Assign team members based on their availability and skill sets
explanation: In Agile projects, high-performing teams benefit from collocation and transparency, which enable learning by osmosis, close collaboration, and faster communication—critical for quick delivery. While diversity of knowledge (A) has merit, collocation is specifically emphasized in Agile as enabling rapid delivery. A detailed resource management plan (B) is more aligned with traditional project management and may slow down the startup. Assigning by availability alone (D) does not ensure team capability for rapid delivery. Agile emphasizes team environment and working relationships over formal resource planning documents.

question: When planning for the team, the project sponsor considers the ability and necessity for team members to work in the same location. What is a key advantage of small project teams that can work in the same room?
options: A. It reduces the need for a project manager
B. It allows the team to take advantage of osmotic communication and solve problems as they arise
C. It eliminates the need for formal communication plans
D. It guarantees project success on the first delivery
explanation: Small teams working in the same location can leverage osmotic communication—overhearing conversations and solving problems together quickly. This reduces communication channels (which increase exponentially with team size) and enables rapid problem-solving. This relates to team composition and structure principles in the PMBOK Guide.

question: You are putting together a project team to deliver an accounting system to officers around the country. The accounting system has not been used by anyone in your organization before. The project is quite high risk and the delivery needs to be right the first time. What will you do next?
options: A. Select a small team that can work in the same area so they can solve problems as they arise
B. Use a predictive project approach and source part of your team externally to access skill sets in the new system
C. Train existing internal staff in the new system and have them work on the project
D. Perform a make or buy analysis on the different software options
explanation: Since the system is new to the organization and the project is high risk with a single delivery requirement, a predictive (waterfall) approach is appropriate. Sourcing part of the team externally brings specialized skills with the new system, which is critical. Training internal staff might work but would introduce team formation delays and lack of expertise in the new system. The make or buy analysis is past the decision point.
topic: Team Composition and Structure, Resource Planning, Project Approach Selection

question: You are putting together a project plan for a change that affects nearly 10,000 stakeholders in your organization. You have identified and prioritized the affected stakeholders and are putting together a communication plan to ensure proper engagement. What will you NOT include in your plan?
options: A. Why information should be shared with stakeholders
B. When and how often information is needed
C. How stakeholders can make changes to the communication plan
D. What is the best way to provide the information
explanation: A communication plan includes: who needs information, what they need, why it should be shared, when and how often it's needed, and the best delivery method. However, the project manager and team develop the communication plan—stakeholders do not typically make changes to it. The project manager incorporates stakeholder feedback, but the stakeholders themselves are not responsible for modifying the communication plan.

question: You put together a resource plan for a large amount of physical inventory to be purchased from overseas. You ordered the inventory during project execution; however, global supply chains were impacted and the shipment was delayed by three months, significantly impacting your project. What should you have done differently?
options: A. Hired a resource manager to take care of resource tasks on the project
B. Planned strategically about the timing from order to delivery to usage, including management of resource risks and their responses
C. Tracked the inventory from arrival on site to delivery of the integrated product
D. Sourced the inventory locally to reduce the impact of global supply chains
explanation: For projects requiring significant physical materials, strategic planning about timing from order to delivery to usage is essential. This includes evaluating logistics, bulk ordering versus storage costs, and supply chain risks. The delay should have been identified as a potential risk during planning and mitigation strategies developed. Tracking inventory after it arrives does not prevent the delay. While sourcing locally might be an option, the question states it must be purchased overseas.
topic: Physical Resources Planning, Risk Management, Resource Scheduling

question: You are halfway through your agile project when a significant change to the project scope is discovered after finding that the project would not be able to deliver the promised benefits. You check your project budget and find that contingency and management reserves are available. What will you do next?
options: A. Take the project scope change to the Change Control Board for approval
B. Use the available contingency reserves to make the appropriate change to the project scope
C. Use the available management reserves to make the appropriate change to the project scope
D. Request additional budget from the project sponsor before proceeding
explanation: Any significant scope change must go through the formal Change Control Board for approval, regardless of available reserves. Contingency reserves are for identified risks, and management reserves are for unforeseen items, but both must be approved through the change control process. Using reserves without proper change control and approval bypasses necessary governance and accountability procedures.
topic: Change Control, Scope Management, Reserve Management

question: You have recently taken over a project that is behind schedule and slightly over budget. You raise an issue at the steering committee meeting where they advise that there is no additional funding available for the project and the project must finish on time. You believe that you can optimize the project process to bring it back on track. What will you do next?
options: A. Use additional people and resources to speed up the project
B. Utilize the Project's contingency reserve to source the resources you need
C. Perform project activities in parallel, completing multiple items at once instead of one at a time
D. Use lean production methods and retrospectives to reduce non-value-added activities and find process improvements
explanation: Lean production methods and retrospectives are specifically focused on process improvement to reduce non-value-added activities. Adding resources may increase complexity and communication channels without truly optimizing processes. Contingency reserve is for project risks, not process optimization. While parallel activities (fast-tracking) can help with scheduling, it is not the most direct way to optimize project processes.

question: Your project management office is optimizing all the projects within their portfolio. They ask for your advice in tracking the type of work each team member is doing and how long it takes, suggesting that they categorize and record their work in addition to the work itself. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask your team to record and categorize their work as it is important
B. Recommend an automated reporting tool where possible, as categorizing their work is non-value-added
C. Ask your team to ignore the request and focus on delivering customer value only
D. Create a manual system for tracking work categories
explanation: Recording and categorizing work is non-value-added activity that does not directly benefit the end customer. While respecting the PMO request, an automated tool can reduce the administrative burden on the team and allow them to focus on actual delivery work. Ignoring the PMO request would harm the relationship with the PMO, and manual systems would still consume unnecessary team time.

question: You are working on an agile project as a Scrum Master where the functional manager has worked with some people in your development team before. The functional manager from the business approaches them directly to see if they can add a feature to the product in their current Sprint. What will you do next?
options: A. Adjust the Sprint backlog to allow for the additional feature
B. Adjust the product backlog to add the customer requirement
C. Ask the manager to speak with the product owner for product priorities, then protect the team from direct requests in the future
D. Ask the manager to create a change request so it can go through the proper process
explanation: In an agile team, the product owner is the single point of decision for prioritizing work and ensuring the highest value work is being done. The functional manager should present the request to the product owner, not directly to the team. As Scrum Master, you have a responsibility to protect the team from external distractions and maintain focus on the current Sprint. This is called being a diversion shield, which prevents the team's productivity from being compromised by outside requests.
topic: Scrum Master Role and Team Protection in Agile

question: Part of the project management plan is a detailed Communications plan which has the stakeholders on the project, their communication needs, and how information will be communicated. While this is being signed off by the project sponsor, you receive many ad hoc requests for information from new stakeholders. What will you do next?
options: A. Review the communications management plan to include the stakeholders with the information that they need exactly as we said and their communication preferences
B. Create an information radiator and ask the stakeholders to pull the information they need
C. Ignore the new requests as you've captured all the stakeholders you need to in your plan
D. Ask the new stakeholders to review your communications management plan to see how you're distributing information
explanation: Once collected information is distributed as indicated in the project management communications plan, an abundance of ad hoc communication requests may indicate that the communications planning was not sufficient to meet stakeholder needs. The best response is to review and update the communications management plan to include the new stakeholders and their communication needs, rather than creating alternative communication methods or ignoring the requests.

question: You are working on a high value construction project to include smart home components within multi-million dollar buildings. After completing a make or buy analysis, you find that it will cost $700,000 to make a new system or $500,000 to buy it from a vendor. You prepare a procurement management plan to purchase it from a vendor. What will you do before conducting a procurement?
options: A. Work with your project team to complete as much as possible in-house as possible
B. Work with contracting professionals in your organization to develop an RFP, statement of work, terms and conditions
C. Adjust your stakeholder register to include the new vendor and add the scope to your work breakdown structure dictionary
D. Work with your project team to develop an RMP, SMP, and list of resources
