explanation: Prior to conducting a procurement, the project manager and technically qualified project team members work with contracting professionals to develop the request for proposal, statement of work, terms and conditions, and other necessary documents to go out to bid. This is the critical step before sending out procurement documents to vendors.

question: A missing item has been uncovered in the project scope that your project team cannot deliver. The item is not complex and seems to be fairly readily available from various sources. You are ready to move to source selection and choose a vendor that will meet your needs. What will you do before sending out those bid documents to vendors?
options: A. Prepare a request for proposal for vendors to provide a solution to your needs
B. Send a request for quote to determine the best price from the vendors
C. Prepare a request for information to gather more information and viable sources from the market
D. Review the procurement management plan for your project's procurement process
explanation: The request for information is used to gather more information from the market prior to sending out bid documents to a set of selected vendors. A request for proposal is used where scope is complex or the buyer is looking for the vendor to provide a solution, and a request for quote is used when price is the deciding factor. Since you need to gather information about viable sources first, the RFI is the appropriate step before sending formal bid documents.

question: You are working on a project where part of the work is planned carefully up front and another part is subject to change. You negotiate terms and conditions including cost, delivery, and payment dates. The vendor is concerned about confusion that might occur between pre-planned and changeable work. What will you do next?
options: A. Use a firm fixed price contract to ensure the cost is fixed but the scope can remain flexible
B. Use a time and materials contract to ensure only the time and materials used are paid for
C. Adjust your project plan to suit either a predictive or adaptive approach to reduce the confusion
D. Use a hybrid contract that clearly separates fixed work from variable work with different pricing structures
explanation: When a project has both pre-planned and changeable work, using a hybrid contract approach that clearly separates and defines the fixed-price portion (for planned work) from the time and materials portion (for changeable work) is the best way to avoid confusion and manage vendor concerns. A firm fixed price contract is too rigid for changeable work, a time and materials contract doesn't protect costs for planned work, and adjusting the project plan to choose one approach defeats the purpose of a mixed methodology.

question: You are working in an agile project where there are vastly differing levels of product knowledge within your team. The Scrum Master suggests that you try to gain knowledge by osmosis because the project information may be lost when only communicating by email. What will you do next?
options: A. Collocate the team to capture the benefit of each person's tacit knowledge
B. Refer to the organizational process assets to capture the team's explicit knowledge
C. Refer to the Project's Knowledge Management System and capture the project's explicit knowledge
D. Create a process to capture the team's tacit knowledge
explanation: Learning by osmosis is capturing information from immediate surroundings such as collocating the team. Tacit knowledge is information that is not straightforward, such as experience, insights, and practical knowledge or skill. This is best captured through collocation where team members naturally overhear conversations and absorb knowledge. Explicit knowledge (options B and C) can be readily codified using words, processes, or pictures, but tacit knowledge requires the team to be together. A process alone (option D) does not capture tacit knowledge as effectively as collocation does.
topic: Knowledge Management and Tacit vs. Explicit Knowledge

question: You are working as a product owner and have placed high-level information about the product in the product backlog. The highest priority feature is at the top. Senior users informing the project are unable to clearly articulate their requirements and are concerned that the product will not truly meet their needs. What will you do next?
options: A. Proceed with the highest priority item during the first Sprint
B. Work with the customer to create mockups and prototypes to discover what works for them
C. Ask your stakeholders to submit a change request and update the project scope accordingly
D. Begin breaking down the work into user stories to place into the Sprint
explanation: When requirements are not clearly defined, prototypes, demonstrations, storyboards, and mockups can be used to evolve those requirements. This approach allows stakeholders to see what the product will look like and say 'I know it when I see it,' enabling them to provide feedback and adjustments before development begins. Proceeding with the Sprint (option A) or breaking down work into user stories (option D) would be premature without clear requirements. Submitting a change request (option C) is not the appropriate response for discovering initial requirements.

question: You are gathering requirements for a new feature in a customer management system. The product owner has given a user interface feature as the next highest priority and you are collocated with the team. What will you do next?
options: A. Show the customer team how the new user interface will look as they will be using it
B. Communicate the requirements to developers with a coherent logical flow of ideas
C. Elicit requirements into user stories with clear, verifiable, and traceable acceptance criteria
D. Show the mockup or prototype to the developers and ask them to develop based on that
explanation: Well-documented requirements are clear, concise, verifiable, consistent, complete, and traceable. Eliciting requirements into user stories with acceptance criteria ensures that developers have all the information needed. While mockups and prototypes are useful (option D), they may not provide sufficient detail for developers to fully understand what needs to be built. Well-articulated user stories with acceptance criteria provide clarity, verifiability, and traceability back to the original requirements, making them the best approach for moving forward.
topic: Requirements Elicitation and User Story Development

question: You are working in an agile team who are trying to break down the project requirements and scope. They have created a scope statement and a work breakdown structure. However, you are not sure how it will work now that they are using Sprints and user stories. What will you do next?
options: A. Assign activities to each of the scope items in the work breakdown structure and place them on a Gantt chart
B. Create a product road map with the team using the lowest most detailed part of the work breakdown structure
C. Convert the work breakdown structure into a Sprint backlog for immediate use
D. Abandon the work breakdown structure and rely solely on user stories
explanation: In an agile environment, a work breakdown structure can still be valuable but should be converted into a product road map. Using the lowest, most detailed parts of the work breakdown structure to create a product road map allows the team to maintain structure while operating within an agile framework with Sprints and user stories. While a Gantt chart (option A) is traditional project management approach that can work in hybrid environments, creating a product road map is the more appropriate agile practice. The work breakdown structure should not be abandoned entirely (option D), and converting directly to a Sprint backlog (option C) may skip important planning steps.
topic: Work Breakdown Structure in Agile Environments

question: Your project team is trialling an agile way of work. The product owner has a clear vision for the product. There is confusion in the team as to how much work should be done on a user story before it's taken to the Sprint review and how they will know that a story is complete. What will you do next?
options: A. Refer to the user story acceptance criteria and your team's definition of done
B. Review the user story with multiple users to ensure it's correct
C. Ask the product owner to sign off on the completed user story
D. Ensure quality testing on the user story is completed and passed
explanation: The definition of done is the key artifact that clarifies when work on a user story is complete. Acceptance criteria and the definition of done together ensure the team knows what 'done' means. While the other options are good practices, they are all components that feed into the acceptance criteria and definition of done rather than the primary mechanism for establishing completion standards.
topic: Agile Ceremonies and Artifacts - Definition of Done

question: You have been working closely with the quality manager of your project planning the budget for the project phase that is due to begin in the next two months. They mention the need to budget for appraisal costs to avoid quality issues in the future. What will you do next?
options: A. Ensure there is enough budget for training
B. Ensure there is enough budget for a quality tester to verify the deliverables against the agreed specifications
C. Ensure there is enough budget to create a quality assurance plan
D. Ensure there is enough budget to allow for rework or rectification when defects are found
explanation: Appraisal costs are the costs of verification and testing activities - inspecting, auditing, and checking deliverables. Option B directly addresses appraisal costs through quality testing and verification. Option A relates to prevention costs, Option C is part of quality planning, and Option D relates to internal failure costs. Appraisal costs occur during the verification and validation phase to detect defects before they reach the customer.

question: Your agile project has recently had multiple quality issues. Project features have been prioritized by the product owner and broken down into user stories. What will you do to help reduce the cost of quality issues the most?
options: A. Quality test each user story according to its acceptance criteria before release
B. Advise the team that any quality defects will come out of their bonus
C. Perform testing all in one go at the end to reduce the cost of testing resources
D. Ensure a triad of developer, tester, and customer is involved during the requirements and design
explanation: The cost of defects is least during requirements and design phases, and increases exponentially as the project progresses - 20 times more expensive during build, 50 times during test, and 150 times if a defect reaches production. By involving developers, testers, and customers early in requirements and design, defects are caught at the source, preventing costly rework later. While option A addresses appraisal costs, involving the triad early prevents defects from occurring in the first place, which is the most cost-effective approach to reducing quality issues.
topic: Cost of Quality - Cost of Change and Early Detection

question: You're working in an agile team where executives in the team you're delivering to are asking for a large display of reports to be created every two weeks showing how the project is tracking and updating them on the cost and benefits. What will you do next?
options: A. Collect the planned value versus earned value to show the executives any variance in the plan
B. Collocate the team with the executives so they can answer any questions as they arise
C. Suggest a demonstration of the feature developed during each Sprint instead and a burndown chart of future product releases
D. Create the desired reports for the executives to keep them happy
explanation: In agile projects, the best approach is to demonstrate actual value and features delivered through Sprint reviews and use live information radiators instead of large reports. This focuses on demonstrating the real customer value and using actual working software rather than just creating reports for reporting's sake. While collocation is good for osmosis learning, demonstrating features with a burndown chart of future product releases is more agile-centric and better addresses the need to show progress and value.

question: Multiple features have been released but they are not having the effect that the product owner believes they should. Your team are using some key performance indicators (KPIs) to track performance and you agree to move to more leading indicators. What could you use as examples of leading indicators on your project?
options: A. An increase in customer usage once the feature is released, such as sales and repeat purchasing
B. Stakeholders who are not engaged and a lack of a risk management process
C. Velocity of the work completed in each Sprint and cycle time of the user stories
D. Variance in the schedule between planned and actual time taken
explanation: Leading indicators predict changes or trends in the project and include factors like lack of a risk management process, stakeholders who are not engaged, and poorly defined project success criteria. These are predictive in nature and will affect future performance. Options A, C, and D are all lagging indicators because they measure work or results that have already happened. Disengaged stakeholders and inadequate risk management processes are indicators that will lead to poor project performance in the future.

question: You're working in an agile project team that has increased in size and complexity since the project began. In reviewing the throughput of work with your team you notice fewer and fewer user stories being completed, and the roadmap of features scheduled to go live is being pushed out. What are you going to do next?
options: A. Place a limit on the work in progress and work with the team to reduce the cycle time
B. Increase meetings with the team
C. Ask the team to work overtime until the number of features reduces
D. Put a pull system in place where the team can pull work only when they are ready
explanation: Limiting work in progress (WIP) helps prevent context switching and multitasking, which are the likely causes of decreased throughput in a larger, more complex team. By focusing on fewer concurrent tasks, the team can complete work faster and reduce cycle time. Option B (more meetings) reduces productivity. Option C (overtime) is anti-agile and unsustainable, leading to burnout. Option D (pull system without WIP limits) could lead to too much work in progress if team members get blocked and pull additional work rather than focusing on unblocking current work.
topic: Agile Team Capacity and Work In Progress Management

question: You are working on an agile project where the project customer wants a reliable way to measure the project performance. You first need to establish a baseline. What measures will you show to your customer as part of your agile project?
options: A. Team velocity
B. Start and finish dates
C. Rate of features completed
D. Schedule and cost variance
explanation: In agile projects, the primary measure shown to customers is the rate of features completed, which demonstrates actual value delivered. Team velocity is an internal team measure not shared with customers. Schedule and cost variance are waterfall concepts; in agile, the schedule and cost are typically fixed while scope is variable. Start and finish dates are less relevant than demonstrating feature delivery.

question: The product owner has worked with a team of senior users to gather feature ideas. They now have a list of more than 50 ideas for your team to develop, which is far too many to complete in the time frame that you have. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask the new product owner to prioritize the features using cost-to-benefit analysis and deliver in their priority order until the time or money runs out
B. Start work on the smallest features first
C. Ask the product owner to return and ask for a shortened list
D. Workshop the features with a wider audience
explanation: The correct approach is to have the product owner prioritize features using business value metrics such as cost-to-benefit ratio, and then deliver in priority order until time or budget is exhausted. Starting with smallest features ignores business value. Asking for a shortened list avoids the prioritization process. Workshopping with a wider audience dilutes the product owner's decision-making authority and slows the process; the product owner should own the prioritization decision.

question: Your agile project team has only recently formed, the product is still unclear, and there have been disagreements and minor conflicts over the past two weeks as the team finds their rhythm. You would like things to improve but you cannot quite pinpoint or address the problem. What will you do next?
options: A. Have the team complete a net promoter score with comments
B. Have the team complete a net promoter score with comments and ask for improvement ideas at the next retrospective
C. Hold a workshop with your team and ask them why they are unhappy
D. Have the team pair up together so they can learn from each other
explanation: The best approach is to use a net promoter score to measure stakeholder satisfaction, gather comments, and then use the retrospective to capture improvement ideas and address root causes. While pairing is an agile practice, it may not address the underlying issues. Workshops focused on complaints without a structured improvement process can be counterproductive. A net promoter score alone without follow-up retrospective action is incomplete.

question: Your project has faced serious impacts in obtaining materials due to supply chain disruptions from a global pandemic and is currently 3 weeks behind schedule. You take this information to the next project steering committee meeting where they advise they need more information on how much the project will end up costing. What will you do next?
options: A. Use the current budget with no deviations until it runs out
B. Use the project burndown chart to see the burn rate of accounting funds
C. Use regression analysis to see the current rate of spending and forecast the final cost
D. Use the project's estimate at completion (EAC)
explanation: Estimate at Completion (EAC) is the most appropriate forecast metric to show the steering committee the likely final project cost. EAC is calculated using the formula: Budget at Completion (BAC) ÷ Cost Performance Index (CPI), where CPI is earned value divided by actual cost. This reflects current performance trends. Regression analysis may work but EAC is the standard earned value forecasting technique. Burndown charts measure feature completion, not cost. Using the current budget with no adjustments does not address the variance caused by delays.
topic: Earned Value Management and Budget Forecasting

question: You are working on an agile project where leaders are spending a considerable amount of time in meetings to stay informed about progress, which reduces time spent on creating the actual product. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask your project sponsor to wait until a steering committee meeting for the information
B. Suggest the leaders come to the Sprint review and gather information from the information radiator
C. Share your working project schedule and budget with the executives instead of creating reports
D. Demonstrate to the customer that you have visible charts and information radiators
explanation: Information radiators (Big Visible Charts) allow stakeholders to pull information anytime without formal meetings. The Sprint review demonstrates actual completed work. This approach is more live and transparent than sharing static reports, and it eliminates the need for frequent status meetings that add non-value-added work.

question: You are working on a project that has planned as much as they can up front, and you would like the team to move to a more agile way of working. Currently the work is hidden, the team catches up only once a week in a working group, and it is hard to see what each person is working on. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask your team to report on their work in a written status update each day so you can keep track
B. Have more working group meetings so you are always up to date on what the team is working on
C. Ask the team to move their work to a central visible task board like a Kanban board and have a short meeting each day to discuss progress on their cards like a standup meeting
D. Remove the team meetings altogether and trust your team to get the work done
explanation: A Kanban board with daily standups creates visibility and transparency while reducing non-value-added work. Standups are short (15 minutes) and focused on removing blockers and ensuring the team has what they need. This is better than daily written updates (which is non-value-added work), more frequent meetings, or removing communication entirely.
topic: Agile Ceremonies and Visual Controls (Kanban Board)

question: You are working on a hybrid project where the majority of the work has been planned up front, the team manages their work via a Kanban board and reports on progress and blockers each day. As the project evolves, stakeholders have added more and more scope to the product, with one executive raising an urgent feature that needs to be added. What will you do next?
options: A. Limit the number of cards in the ready or to-do column and ask the product owner to work with stakeholder requests, noting the need to swap the new item for an existing item
B. Match the number of user stories to the team's velocity to ensure a sustainable pace
C. Work with the stakeholder to raise a change request and note the outcome in a change log
D. Review the impact of the scope change with the stakeholder using the work breakdown structure dictionary
explanation: In a hybrid Kanban environment, work-in-progress limits and prioritization control scope creep. The product owner must help stakeholders understand that adding new features means deprioritizing existing items. This maintains sustainable pace and ensures the team works on the highest-value items. A change log is less appropriate for agile scope management, and the WBS dictionary is not used for impact reviews.

question: You are working as a product owner in an agile team where the main measure of project success is the number of features delivered into production. Over the past 6 months, the team has delivered the highest number of features your organization has ever seen. However, customers are now starting to complain about issues and bugs in the product. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask the team to work harder to deliver more features and fix product issues
B. Adjust the product metrics to include customer satisfaction and quality measures alongside feature delivery
C. Add a slack card of 5 points in the Sprint backlog to deal with technical debt
D. Review the product roadmap and reduce the number of planned features
explanation: Adjusting success metrics to include quality and customer satisfaction will change team behavior and priorities. What gets measured gets managed; by expanding metrics to include quality measures, the team will naturally balance feature velocity with product quality. While technical debt management (option C) is important, it addresses the symptom rather than the root cause of misaligned incentives.

question: You have been working on a project for the past 8 months. Your project team has been approached by multiple stakeholders for reporting on various performance measures about the project and the product area. A large amount of their time is spent on creating these reports for others, and they have escalated this issue to you. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask the project stakeholders to stop interacting with your team.
B. Spend a portion of your project budget to automate the reports for your stakeholders.
C. Work with the team, including the stakeholders, to only measure things that will facilitate a decision, help avoid an issue, or increase project performance.
D. Invite your stakeholders to your Sprint reviews so they can see a working feature in practice.
explanation: The correct answer addresses the root cause of the measurement issue by ensuring that only relevant metrics that facilitate decisions, prevent issues, or improve performance are measured and reported. This maintains stakeholder engagement while reducing unnecessary reporting burden. The Hawthorne Effect teaches that what we measure influences team behavior, so measuring only meaningful metrics balances team behavior and prevents wasted effort on non-value-added reporting. Automating reports (B) doesn't address the root cause, inviting stakeholders to Sprint reviews (D) is good practice but doesn't solve the core problem, and cutting off stakeholder interaction (A) eliminates necessary engagement.
topic: Measurement Pitfalls and the Hawthorne Effect

question: You are working on a project where there is a significant amount of uncertainty about the requirements. There is also a legislative change coming and no one knows what it will mean for your industry. As a project manager, how do you deal with this uncertainty?
options: A. Perform additional planning for your project to improve your view on scope management.
B. Hold additional retrospectives to capture challenges and improvements from your team.
C. Create a risk assessment of all possible project risks, maybe with their risk ratings and owners.
D. Gather information and prepare for multiple outcomes, and build team resilience through processes and training.
explanation: The correct answer addresses uncertainty through multiple approaches: gathering information, preparing for multiple outcomes, and building team resilience. This comprehensive approach tackles uncertainty from different angles. Additional planning alone (A) cannot eliminate inherent uncertainty. Retrospectives (B) don't help address future uncertainty directly. While risk assessment (C) is useful, it doesn't encompass preparation for multiple outcomes or team resilience building. The best approach combines gathering information, preparing contingencies, and building the team's capability to work effectively in uncertain environments.

question: You are working on a project with a vendor where there is significant ambiguity involved. Many outcomes are possible with varying degrees of impact. What should you do to improve the situation?
options: A. Use experiments and prototypes and progressive elaboration to work your way through it.
B. Ask your team for additional reporting around different options so you can make an informed decision.
C. Add all the different options into your project scope so all your bases are covered.
D. Work more closely with your senior users so they can inform you of the product requirements.
explanation: The correct answer uses experiments, prototypes, and progressive elaboration to navigate ambiguity by iteratively learning which options are viable. This approach is ideal when there are multiple possible outcomes—you test them in small increments, gather feedback, and adjust accordingly rather than trying to decide everything upfront. Adding all options to scope (C) doesn't resolve the ambiguity; it merely adds complexity. Additional reporting (B) and closer user engagement (D), while potentially helpful, do not directly address the fundamental ambiguity or provide a mechanism to work through multiple possible outcomes effectively.

question: You are working on a project that integrates with many other systems within your organization. The system is quite complex, and you cannot decide which features to deliver first. How will you deal with the complexity?
options: A. List out the possible features you can deliver and prioritize them by cost over benefit
B. Simulate decoupling connecting parts to simplify the number of variables
C. Reframe your view by brainstorming with a diverse set of stakeholders
D. Provide an incentive for your project team for whoever can solve this complex problem
explanation: Methods for dealing with complexity include systems-based approaches such as decoupling and simulation. This allows you to test scenarios without physically implementing changes. While brainstorming with diverse stakeholders and prioritization are good practices, simulation of decoupling is the most direct technical approach to reducing complexity. Providing incentives alone does not address the systemic complexity. Reference: PMBOK Guide 7th Edition, page 121, Complexity section.

question: You are working on a project that requires a very particular programming skill set that is not very common. The job market is the hottest the industry has seen in two decades, people are hard to come by and leave often, and the programming language is evolving at a quick pace. What will you do next?
options: A. Develop the product from scratch in a more common programming language
B. Ask your whole team to learn the programming skill set to cover the risk
C. Use alternatives analysis to gather different options and ensure a sufficient cost reserve to help complete your project
D. Raise a risk to your project completion with the threat of project team member skill sets
explanation: This scenario describes volatility, an environment subject to rapid and unpredictable change, including ongoing fluctuations in available skill sets. The best response to volatility is to use alternatives analysis to explore different options and establish cost reserves as contingencies. While raising the risk is appropriate, alternatives analysis combined with cost reserves represents a more mature and pragmatic approach to dealing with volatility. Reference: PMBOK Guide 7th Edition, page 122, Volatility section.
topic: Volatility Management and Risk Response Planning

question: Your project team has found a significant data vulnerability in one of the features planned in your product backlog where customer data is exposed without password access. The feature is partially made by a third-party vendor, and the relationship is owned by a functional manager in your organization. What will you do next?
options: A. Avoid the risk and wait for the third-party vendor to fix the vulnerability
B. Transfer the risk by asking a different vendor to create a feature with no vulnerability in their design
C. Escalate the risk to the manager with the appropriate authority to act on the response
D. Accept the risk and move on to other features
explanation: Escalation is the appropriate risk response when the project team agrees that the threat is outside the scope of the project or exceeds the project manager's authority. In this case, the risk is severe, the vendor relationship is owned by a functional manager, and the project manager lacks direct authority. Escalation allows those with appropriate authority to manage this significant security threat. Accepting or avoiding such a critical vulnerability is inappropriate, and transferring the risk may take too long. Reference: PMBOK Guide 7th Edition, page 123, Overall Project Risks and Threats section.

question: You are working on a project to build a new customer database system for an engineering team. While working with the requirements, you realize that this system can also work with other teams in your organization and the effort to add those teams is actually quite small. What will you do next?
options: A. Escalate it to management for approval
B. Exploit the opportunity to actively ensure it happens
C. Enhance the opportunity by increasing its probability of occurrence
D. Accept the opportunity and acknowledge it without taking additional action
explanation: Exploit is the correct response to positive risks/opportunities when you want to act to ensure an opportunity occurs. Escalating applies when you lack authority. Enhancing increases probability but doesn't actively make it happen. Accepting merely acknowledges the opportunity without taking action. In this scenario, you have the authority and capability to add the other teams, so exploiting the opportunity is the best response.

question: You are working as a product owner in an agile team. During a recent risk review, the team has uncovered various risks that will impact your project. You brainstorm risk responses with your team. The project customer would like them to be mitigated as soon as possible. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask your team to work on the risk responses and get them done as soon as possible
B. Ignore the risks initially because some might dissipate over time and only the important ones will be left
C. Assign the risk responses to all stakeholders around your project team to share the load
D. Prioritize the risks by comparing the expected monetary value of the risk to the anticipated return on investment
explanation: In agile environments, prioritizing risks using a risk-adjusted backlog approach by comparing expected monetary value of risks to anticipated return on investment of deliverables allows product owners to incorporate risk responses into planned work appropriately. Simply asking the team to do it ignores other work priorities. Ignoring risks allows them to potentially worsen. Assigning to all stakeholders indiscriminately creates inefficiency. The correct approach integrates risk management into the normal prioritization process.

question: You are planning a project where supply chains have been impacted and the requirements are complex and intertwined with other areas of the business. Your project sponsor asks you to manage these risks before the project moves to execution. What will you do next?
options: A. Move to project execution so you can work through risks as they happen
B. Move to an agile way of work so you can deliver faster and deal with the complexity
C. Note the risks with appropriate controls and add a contingency reserve in the project budget
D. Assign a management reserve to the project budget
explanation: Adding a contingency reserve to the project budget is the appropriate way to manage identified risks before execution. Contingency reserves are set aside for identified risks should they occur, while management reserves are for unknown, unplanned in-scope work. Simply moving to execution or switching methodologies does not address risk management. Management reserves are not the appropriate tool for identified, known risks.

question: Your project management office has no prescribed way of work so they're working with a vendor who supplies an extreme programming framework at a significant cost. However, projects are still running behind schedule, over budget, and with low customer satisfaction. What should you do next?
options: A. Tailor the approach to suit the organization operating environment and project needs
B. Use a waterfall approach instead to ensure the proper planning
C. Switch to scrum instead as it's a more popular framework
D. Tighten the controls around the project environment with a more directive PMO approach
explanation: Tailoring is performed to better suit the organization operating environment and project needs. Projects operate in complex environments that need to balance competing demands. Simply switching frameworks or tightening controls without addressing the underlying fit between the framework and the organization's context will not solve the problem.

question: Your project management office is in the middle of a companywide ways of working transformation. They have taken a popular project framework and are tailoring it to the organization with branded templates and processes that meet their existing governance requirements. You've been asked to help finalize the process. What will you do next?
options: A. Gather feedback from the functional business areas to ensure the process meets their needs
B. Roll out the new way of working to the PMO as a pilot then to the rest of the organization
C. Enable a process for tailoring based on size and complexity and implement ongoing improvement
D. Form a working group comprised of executives and project professionals
explanation: The tailoring process follows these steps: select the initial development approach, tailor for the organization, tailor for the project, and implement ongoing improvement. Enabling a process for tailoring based on size and complexity with ongoing improvement captures the complete iterative nature of organizational transformation.

question: You're a manager of a PMO in a large organization which has recently undertaken a companywide way of working transformation. You need to select an initial development approach for your project which you will then tailor to suit your organization and project needs. What will you do next?
options: A. Use a framework that best engages your executive team through easy to use graphics and presentations
B. Select the most popular current framework to keep up with what other organizations are doing
C. Select multiple development approaches as a baseline for your organization
D. Use a suitability filter to assess the most appropriate development approach
explanation: A suitability filter helps project teams consider whether a project has characteristics that lend themselves towards a predictive, hybrid, or adaptive approach. This allows you to select one as a base and then enable the ability to tailor to your project or organization's circumstances. Relying on graphics, popularity, or multiple frameworks without a structured assessment will not result in the best fit for your organization.

question: Your organization is starting to move to an agile way of work and they're setting up a value delivery office (VDO) to complement the existing PMO. The executives in the area have begun working through what the function looks like and the roles and responsibilities that might exist. They approach you for help. What will you tell them about the VDO's primary focus?
options: A. The VDO is responsible for the value delivered and owns the programs within the organization
B. The VDO focuses on coaching, building adaptive skills, growing team capabilities, and mentoring sponsors and product owners
C. The VDO is responsible for allocating agile resources such as scrum masters and product owners effectively to project teams
D. The VDO creates the organizational framework to be used on each project throughout the company
explanation: In an agile environment, a value delivery office focuses on coaching, facilitation, and mentoring rather than directing. It builds adaptive skills, grows team capabilities, and mentors sponsors and product owners to be more effective. This coaching and enabling approach is distinct from the more directive PMO functions described in the other options.
topic: Agile Organizational Roles and Value Delivery

question: You have been planning a project for the last 2 months, gathering requirements, scope, and assigning tasks to a schedule. As you get closer to the execution phase of your project, you decide what delivery approach you will use. The requirements are likely to change, require a medium amount of compliance, but can be released in one go. What delivery approach will you use?
options: A. Predictive
B. Incremental
C. Agile or adaptive
D. Iterative
explanation: An iterative delivery approach involves gathering feedback and improving over time while delivering in one release. Incremental releases deliver increments of value like features over time. Predictive plans everything up front and then delivers in one go. Agile gathers feedback and releases incrementally. This scenario describes iterating towards success with a single release, which is the defining characteristic of an iterative approach.

question: You have been asked to make a process improvement in the accounting area for your organization. You have been assigned a small team of nine people, each with all the skills you need to complete the project. The team is collocated and has unlimited access to the project customer. What delivery approach will you use?
options: A. Predictive
B. Agile or adaptive
C. Incremental
D. Hybrid
explanation: An agile or adaptive delivery approach works best when the team is small (fewer than 9 to 12 people), has close access to the customer, is cross-functional (everyone has the required skills), and is collocated in the same space. Predictive is best when lots of planning is needed. Hybrid is best when two different approaches are needed. Incremental delivers features over time but is not the best fit for this scenario.

question: You have been working as a project manager in an organization for some time where an agile-centric delivery approach and way of work has been selected and tailored for the organization. Over the past few months, you notice several things are not working and could be improved as you begin new projects. What will you do next?
options: A. Review the product backlog to see the flow of features going through the teams
B. Look into issues, threats, and quality assurance statistics
C. Hold a retrospective with your PMO leaders to determine ongoing improvement opportunities
D. Review a control chart and value stream map to see non-value-added activities and remove waste
explanation: In an agile environment, holding a retrospective together to discuss what is working well and what is challenging or could be improved is the best way to find ongoing improvements. This involves going directly to the source and asking for feedback from the team. While reviewing control charts and value stream maps can be part of problem-solving, a retrospective is the primary agile practice for continuous improvement. This approach is found in the PBOK Guide 7th Edition, page 151, under Tailoring Diagnostics for Agile Teams.

question: You have been working on a project to deliver a new customer data system and have noticed that the work is starting to slow down. Fewer user stories are appearing in your sprint backlog as they are not ready. You hold a retrospective with your team and discover that there are long delays waiting for requirements and acceptance criteria approvals. What will you do next?
options: A. Streamline approval decisions through fewer people, such as a product owner authorized to make decisions up to certain value thresholds
B. Ask your team to place the user stories in the sprint backlog, reducing the need for customer approval
C. Note the long delays as a risk to your project with high likelihood
D. Escalate the issue to the functional manager to ensure approvals are happening quickly
explanation: When there are long delays waiting for approvals, the tailoring suggestion is to streamline decisions through fewer people authorized to make decisions up to certain value thresholds. This empowers the product owner to make timely decisions without excessive bureaucracy. Asking the team to bypass customer approval would compromise quality. While escalating may be an option, it does not directly solve the root problem. This tailoring guidance comes directly from the PBOK Guide 7th Edition, page 151, under Common Situations and Tailoring Suggestions.

question: Your agile team is experiencing too much work in progress and high rates of scrap. What is the best recommendation to address this issue?
options: A. Add more people to the project to complete the work faster
B. Use value stream mapping and Kanban boards to visualize the work and identify issues
C. Ask your team to work overtime to cover the extra work
D. Review the scope of the project and reduce items with low customer impact
explanation: When there is too much work in progress or high rates of scrap, the recommended approach is to use techniques like value stream mapping and Kanban boards to visualize the work, identify the issues, and propose solutions. This allows teams to limit work in progress and reduce context switching. Adding more people can complicate communication and processes. Asking for overtime is unsustainable and leads to disengagement. While scope review is a decent practice, it is not the best answer for addressing work-in-progress issues. This guidance is found in the PMBOK Guide, 7th Edition, page 151 under common situations and tailoring suggestions.
topic: Agile Work Management and Process Improvement

question: Your product owner has prioritized the product backlog and the team has created user stories with estimates filling a two-week Sprint. However, you notice that pieces being completed are being rejected by the customer due to defects and missed requirements. What should you do next?
options: A. Create the project scope from scratch with the project team and customers
B. Have an iteration zero planning Sprint with your project team to set it up for success
C. Add more feedback loops such as shoulder checks, peer review, unit testing, and system testing
D. Have less frequent customer demonstrations so they only see the product when it is correct
explanation: When a product is facing poor quality deliverables, the recommended tailoring suggestion is to add more feedback loops and quality assurance steps. These include demonstrations and reviews, peer review of items, shoulder checks, pair programming, quality audits, and various levels of testing such as unit testing, system testing, user acceptance testing, regression testing, and automated testing. By catching issues earlier through multiple feedback loops, especially at the requirements stage, costs are minimized. Creating scope from scratch is excessive, iteration zero delays value delivery, and less frequent demonstrations reduce customer feedback opportunities. This guidance is found in the PMBOK Guide, 7th Edition, page 151.
topic: Quality Assurance and Feedback Loops in Agile

question: Your project team has started to find their rhythm, but you notice they seem disengaged and are dragging their feet on normal tasks. You check the budget and confirm the team is paid fairly for the market and within the same range for the role. What is the best way to improve engagement?
options: A. Look for ways to reward team members with bonuses above their normal pay rate
B. Move team members around to different roles frequently so they experience job variety
C. Give the team a paid day off
D. Establish ways for the team to grow and advance
explanation: According to Herzberg's motivational theory, once hygiene factors such as fair compensation and physical environment are satisfied, people are motivated by factors such as achievement, growth, and advancement. While bonuses, role variety, and paid time off may provide temporary relief, they wear off quickly. The most effective long-term approach is to establish career paths, learning pathways, and opportunities for growth and advancement that give team members a sense of progress. This taps into the intrinsic motivational factors that sustain engagement beyond basic compensation.
topic: Team Motivation and Herzberg's Motivational Theory

question: You are working as a project manager on a regulatory change for your organization. You notice that the team is not communicating as well as they could be and you put together a plan to improve team communication by increasing its effectiveness and richness. What will you do?
options: A. Include methods that will handle multiple information cues such as body language, voice, and words
B. Facilitate rapid feedback to ensure everyone is on the same page
C. Utilize natural language avoiding jargon or other complicated language
D. Include ways to get sign-off in writing so it can be recorded and reviewed later when needed
explanation: According to Alistair Cockburn's Crystal method and media richness theory, effectiveness and richness of communication is achieved through methods that handle multiple information cues (body language, voice, words), facilitate rapid feedback, establish personal focus, and utilize natural language. Option A directly addresses the core principle of media richness by including methods to handle multiple information cues, which is the foundation of effective communication channels.

question: Your deliverables have been signed off by the product owner and they are ready to be released to the customer, which is the financial analytics team of your organization. The new system will be quite a change from their normal day-to-day work. The team already has the desire to use the new system. What will you do next?
options: A. Develop a vision and explain the why behind the change
B. Use training and education to show them the new processes and systems
C. Give them unlimited hands-on practice so they can get used to the new system
D. Include rewards, recognition, feedback, and measurement once the system is released
explanation: According to the ADCAR model for change management (Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement), once people have the desire for change, the next step is knowledge, which includes training and education for the new processes and systems. Since the team already has the desire to use the system, the appropriate next step is to provide training and education rather than building desire or providing rewards after release.

question: You are leading a large organizational change that affects more than a thousand people across multiple teams. You have engaged with the senior managers of that area, created a brief vision statement that summarizes the change, and created a strategy to realize the vision. You are communicating the vision throughout the change process. What will you do next?
options: A. Release multiple usable features to the affected teams so they can get used to it gradually
B. Anchor the changes into corporate culture and recognize people for their efforts
C. Address any obstacles to the change, including outdated processes and people resistance
D. Set goals for continued improvement on the change throughout the organization
explanation: According to Kotter's Eight-Step Process for Leading Change, after communicating the vision comes the step of removing obstacles. This includes addressing outdated processes and people resistance before proceeding with implementation. Anchoring changes into corporate culture comes later in the process after short-term wins have been achieved. Releasing features or setting improvement goals should come after obstacles have been addressed.
topic: Organizational Change and Kotter's Eight-Step Process

question: You are a project manager assessing the risk of future releases with your team. You have a clear set of requirements from your customer; however, there are a range of possible correct solutions. There are also a range of known unknowns where you know that risks exist but the impact is uncertain. How are you going to approach this situation?
options: A. Use current best practices to make decisions
B. Use emergent practices that will allow you to probe the environment, sense the situation, and respond with action
C. Assess the facts, analyze the situation, and apply known good practices
D. Sense where there is some stability and take steps to try and stabilize the situation
explanation: This situation describes the Cynefin framework's complicated domain, where there is a set of known unknowns and a range of correct answers. In the complicated domain, the appropriate approach is to assess the facts, analyze the situation, and apply known good practices. Option B (emergent practices and probing) applies to the complex domain, while Option D applies to the chaotic domain. Option A alone is insufficient because analysis is needed to determine which best practices are most appropriate.

question: You have been asked to initiate a project to deliver a new system. The requirements are not clear and it will take some time to uncover them. The system has been used by some team members but not all and it has a medium level of uncertainty around it. How are you going to approach this project?
options: A. Release small usable features and improve your approach based on feedback
B. Do additional planning up front to ensure every component is thought of
C. Split the team into two and work on different parts of the system to simplify it
D. Form a business case for starting from scratch with new easier-to-use technology
explanation: When requirements are unclear and there is medium uncertainty, agile methodologies work best. Releasing small usable features allows teams to work through uncertainty and make requirements clearer through feedback. This aligns with the Stacy Matrix, which shows that agile methodologies work best in complicated and complex environments. Additional upfront planning (B) will not help resolve the inherent uncertainty. Splitting teams (C) reduces collaboration when it is needed most. Starting from scratch (D) is not practical and delays value delivery.

question: Your project team came together from different parts of the organization and were introduced formally to each other. You notice that there is some conflict in the team. Some people do not respect others' decisions and are not working together well, and there are some personality clashes within the team. What should you do next?
options: A. Help team members get to know each other and their names
B. Help the team understand their roles and responsibilities and form a regular rhythm of meetings, collaboration, and work
C. Help the team jockey for position within the team and foster healthy conflict
D. Help the team complete project work and disperse onto other things
explanation: This scenario describes the storming stage of the Tuckman ladder, where conflict and difficulty in working together emerge. The appropriate intervention is to help the team understand their roles and responsibilities and establish a regular rhythm so they can move to the norming stage. Option A is premature since introductions have already occurred. Option C suggests fostering unhealthy competition rather than resolution. Option D is appropriate only after the project is complete. The key is to move the team from storming to norming.

question: You have formed a new project team and have worked through the purpose and mission of the project with them. You have expanded on who is on the project team and the skills and abilities each person brings. You would like to foster a high performing culture and ensure the team is able to deliver as soon as possible. What are you going to do next?
options: A. Work through changes on the project team and the projects such as deliverables and stakeholders
B. Decompose the high level plan into greater detail including a detailed schedule or backlog
C. Define a plan to achieve the project goals including milestone schedules, release plans, and high level budgets
D. Clarify the goals of the team and what you are here to deliver
explanation: This scenario follows the Drexler Sibbet team performance model. After orientation (why and who), the next step is goal clarification (what), which clarifies the goals of the team and what they are here to deliver. Only after goal clarification comes commitment and the detailed planning phases. Option B (decompose into backlog) and Option C (define plan) occur after goal clarification. Option A is premature. The correct sequence is: orientation → trust building → goal clarification → commitment → implementation → high performance → renewal.

question: Your team members have raised an issue as they believe the current project scope will not reach the intended project goal of increasing customer attention. You take their concerns to the project sponsor who controls the funding and resources. The sponsor dismisses them and directs you to proceed with the scope currently set. What will you do next?
options: A. Problem solve with the sponsor and find a resolution
B. Compromise with the sponsor so each party gets a little bit of what they want
C. Accommodate the sponsor to maintain harmony and goodwill
D. Force the issue so the sponsor accepts the change
explanation: This scenario involves conflict resolution between the project manager and the sponsor. According to the Thomas-Kilman conflict resolution model, different approaches are appropriate depending on the situation. Accommodating (C) is appropriate here because the sponsor controls funding and resources and is the ultimate decision-maker for project direction. While problem-solving (A) and compromising (B) are generally valuable approaches, they are less appropriate when the other party has formal authority. Forcing (D) is a win-lose approach that damages the relationship with the sponsor and can harm the project manager's career. Accommodation is a reasonable choice given the sponsor's authority and the importance of maintaining the relationship.

question: A functional manager raises a concern that they have not been engaged correctly and the program is missing some key requirements as a result. You believe you've captured all the items required. Your program manager asks you to handle the situation and approach it from a win-win perspective. What will you do next?
options: A. Escalate the decision to the steering committee to ensure your point of view is heard
B. Demonstrate integrity, find mutual trust, and look at the situation from their point of view
C. Choose to give some leeway in your approach so the functional manager can add their requirements
D. Get your requirements baselined first and then compete with the manager to ensure a win
explanation: A win-win approach requires mature character, trust, and the ability to approach the situation from the other's point of view. This is directly from the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition page 169 under negotiation and aligns with Stephen Covey's principle of 'think win-win.' While the other options may seem reasonable, only option B encompasses all the elements necessary for a true win-win resolution that maintains the important relationship with the functional manager.

question: You have been planning a project to deliver a process change for the past 4 weeks. The requirements and scope have been signed off by the business owner and the team are moving into delivery and execution. The work begins in line with the project plan and scope. What will you do next?
options: A. Monitor and control the project work
B. Execute the project work
C. Plan the work
D. Formally sign off the project
explanation: The project has moved into the executing phase. As project work is executed, it must then be monitored and controlled to ensure it stays on track and meets its goals. The project management process groups are initiating, planning, executing, monitoring and controlling, and closing. These may be performed in sequence or iterated, but once execution begins, monitoring and controlling is the concurrent activity. This is from the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition page 170 under process groups.

question: You are working as a product owner. While your sales and revenue have been increasing over the past 8 months, the overall profit has decreased by 17%. Your executive manager asks you to address the situation. What are you going to do next?
options: A. Stop the current work and set a planning session with your team to address the situation
B. Ask your team to work overtime on the current features in your backlog to get the work done
C. Perform benchmarking with a similar yet non-competitive company and add any process improvements to your product backlog
D. Look for opportunities to partner with other projects and products in your organization and increase your sales further
explanation: The core issue is that profit is decreasing while sales are increasing, which means costs are too high. Performing benchmarking to find gaps in your process and then executing improvements with your team will help reduce costs and improve profitability. Option D would worsen the problem by increasing sales without addressing the root cause of rising costs. Options A and B would be disruptive and unsustainable. As a product owner, your responsibility includes increasing profit. This is from the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition page 175 under data gathering and analysis and benchmarking.

question: You are creating a business case and a project charter with a small initial project team. You have a high-level feature decomposed into smaller release milestones and you'd like to note a high-level schedule using wideband delphi or a high-level version of planning poker. What are you going to do next?
options: A. Hold a round of cards with points on them where each team member places their cards in the center for the total estimate
B. Ask subject matter experts to complete multiple rounds of estimates individually, compare and explain the highest and lowest estimates for each round
C. Show the wideband high-level range from the lowest to highest for each feature
D. Find the business functionality for each feature and estimate the effort based on that
explanation: The wideband delphi technique asks subject matter experts to complete multiple rounds of estimates individually, compare and explain the highest and lowest estimates for each round. Planning poker is a variation of wideband delphi. The other options describe different estimation approaches or incomplete versions of the process.

question: You have been asked to help facilitate a senior planning meeting in your organization. High-level executives have made a presentation on the strengths and opportunities of the organization. Revenue has fallen recently and the chief executive asks you to level out their analysis with a more complete view. What will you do next?
options: A. Add your organization's competitors and compare their products
B. Showcase the pipeline of upcoming work to be delivered in the next quarter
C. Add the background and abilities of the executive team that will allow the organization to deliver
D. Brainstorm and capture the weaknesses of the organization and threats to the organization
explanation: This question refers to SWOT analysis (strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, threats). Since strengths and opportunities have been captured, a complete picture also involves the weaknesses and threats to provide a balanced view. The other options do not complete the SWOT analysis framework.

question: You are adding an essential feature to your product roadmap. Your team believes they can complete the feature in 6 weeks. However, the feature is quite large and a bit more complicated than normal. One of your lead developers says they worked on a similar feature in another company and it took around 4 months to complete. What will you do next?
options: A. Use relative estimating
B. Use analogous estimating and place the feature with around 4 months to complete
C. Use parametric estimating
D. Use function point estimating to show the amount of business functionality in the feature
explanation: When the effort on a feature or deliverable needs to be estimated and there are people who have experience delivering a similar item, you can use analogous estimating. Since the lead developer has worked on a similar feature in the past that took 4 months, this provides an analogy to estimate the effort quickly with a medium level of accuracy. The other options describe different estimation techniques that are not appropriate for this scenario.

question: Your team is working on estimating user stories for an upcoming sprint. Which approach would be most effective for helping the team perform relative estimating?
options: A. Ask each team member to add their own estimates as they complete the acceptance criteria
B. Estimate the effort for the overall feature and reduce the need for individual story estimates
C. Have the team size stories in points relative to the smallest piece, often using a Fibonacci scale, with input from all team members including testers and developers
D. Base the estimates on cost per hour and add them up after the work is done to ensure accuracy
explanation: Relative estimating is the most common method for user stories where they are sized in comparison to the smallest piece, often in story points and frequently using a Fibonacci scale. This approach benefits from collaborative input from all team members involved in the work (developers, testers, etc.), which is a key advantage over individual estimating. The other options either miss the collaborative aspect, don't address the need for story-level estimates for sprint velocity calculation, or confuse estimating with actual cost tracking.

question: You are halfway through your current sprint of two weeks. The work is progressing well, however you notice that there is not enough work ready and elaborated for when the next sprint begins. What will you do next?
options: A. Hold a backlog refinement meeting and work with the team to identify the work that could be accomplished
B. Raise the issue as a blocker in the daily standup and ask your team to swarm around the problem
C. Raise the issue in the next retrospective and take an action for the team
D. Place the cards on the kanban board so the team can see the work and get started
explanation: A backlog refinement meeting is the appropriate action to take during the current sprint to progressively elaborate and prioritize the backlog for the upcoming iteration. The team needs to identify work that meets the definition of ready before the next sprint begins. A retrospective occurs at the end of a sprint, making it too late to prepare for the upcoming sprint. The daily standup is not the appropriate forum for this type of planning, and work must be ready according to the definition of ready before being placed on a board.

question: You have completed a make-or-buy analysis in your project and found that part of your deliverable is best created by an external vendor. You work with your project team to create the procurement strategy, source selection criteria, and statement of work. What should you do next?
options: A. Gather the seller proposals including technical details and price
B. Meet with prospective sellers to ensure all vendors have a common understanding of the procurement
C. Perform source selection analysis on what you want from prospective vendors
D. Create the procurement agreement for the third-party vendor to provide their specified services
explanation: A bidder conference (also called a contractor conference, vendor conference, or pre-bid conference) is an output of plan procurement management where you meet with prospective sellers prior to the preparation of a bid or proposal to ensure all prospective bidders have a clear and common understanding of the procurement. This meeting comes after you have established your procurement strategy and source selection criteria, but before you gather actual proposals. Source selection analysis occurs later, and the procurement agreement is created only after a seller has been selected.

question: One of the stakeholders in your project has raised a change request after some of the deliverables contained defects and a missed requirement. Your project has a formal change control process in place. What will you do next?
options: A. Gather feedback from other stakeholders to see if they also need the change
B. Note the change in the change log and place the requested item in the next sprint
C. Take it to the change control board and communicate the decision to approve or reject the change
D. Raise the request as an issue that will affect your project schedule and budget
explanation: In a formal change control process, the change request must be submitted to the change control board, which includes people who are accountable for reviewing, approving, or rejecting changes to the project. The decisions made at this meeting are recorded and communicated to appropriate stakeholders. Simply noting the change in a log or placing it in the next sprint bypasses the formal control process. While a change may affect schedule and budget, that is not the immediate next step—the change must first go through the formal review and approval process.
topic: Change Control and Formal Change Management

question: Your agile project has recently gained some new stakeholders after a department restructure. They complain to you that they are not seeing enough traction of work delivered through the project and they are worried that it will not meet their requirements. What should you do next?
options: A. Invite them to the daily standup so they can see the work that happens on a daily basis
B. Invite them to iteration planning so they can choose what the team works on
C. Schedule a backlog refinement session to understand their specific requirements
D. Provide them with a detailed project schedule and budget forecast
explanation: Inviting new stakeholders to iteration planning (sprint planning) allows them to understand what work will be done in the upcoming sprint and ensures their requirements and priorities are being considered. This helps address their concerns about traction and requirements being met. While the daily standup can be open to stakeholders in some methodologies, iteration planning is the more appropriate venue for stakeholder involvement in prioritization decisions. The daily standup's primary purpose is for the development team to coordinate work, whereas iteration planning is where priorities and requirements are discussed. Backlog refinement may be helpful, but iteration planning more directly addresses their concerns about work selection and priority.

question: You have recently taken over a project. Your project team approached you to say that there is a regulatory approval that has not been granted yet and it might block development of your project in the next 5 months. You check your project documentation but cannot see any mention of this. What will you do next?
options: A. Hold a risk review meeting, capture it as a risk, and ensure an adequate risk response
B. Hold a retrospective with your team to capture what challenges the team are facing
C. Adjust your product roadmap to reflect the changes to the timeframe of that deliverable
D. Escalate the regulatory approval to the project steering committee
explanation: This is a future event that may or may not happen—a classic definition of a risk before it becomes an issue. The best approach is to hold a risk review meeting to capture it as a risk, analyze it, and determine an adequate risk response. Escalating it removes the project manager's responsibility to manage the risk, retrospectives are for improving team processes rather than identifying and managing risks, and adjusting the roadmap prematurely is not advisable when the regulatory approval status is uncertain.

question: You are an agile project manager holding daily standups with your team. You notice people accusing other team members of not doing their job and there are some tasks that are not mentioned anywhere and no one is responsible for. The team velocity has slowed in the last three Sprints. What will you do next?
options: A. Refer to the team charter to clarify team roles and agreed ways of managing conflict
B. Raise it as an issue in the next retrospective and come to a solution with the team
C. Assign team roles to those you think are best suited
D. Work with your team to size user stories correctly so velocity improves
explanation: Since tasks are not mentioned anywhere and roles are not defined in the team charter, referring to the charter would not help. The agile approach is to problem-solve with the team in a retrospective—a regularly occurring workshop where the team explores their work to improve their process and product. This allows the team to collaboratively identify missing tasks, clarify responsibilities, and address conflicts. While assigning roles unilaterally might seem efficient, agile emphasizes team collaboration and self-organization.

question: You are managing a large construction project. Due to some delays, the project schedule has been pushed back by one week. As a result, one of the project risks has increased slightly but is still within the acceptable limits. What will you do next?
options: A. Hold a risk review and update the risk response and owners
B. Raise a change request for the change to the project schedule
C. Capture the change in your project reporting and take it to the next status meeting
D. Update the Sprint backlog to reflect the delayed work
explanation: Since the risk has increased slightly but remains within acceptable limits, there is no immediate need to update the risk response or owners. A change request is unnecessary as the schedule change has already occurred. Updating the Sprint backlog is not applicable to a large construction project. The best practice is to capture the change and communicate it through your status meeting to keep stakeholders informed about project progress and performance. Communication and transparency are key components of project management.

question: A product owner prioritizes features to improve usability and profitability of a product. Which measure would best help the product owner understand customer satisfaction and loyalty?
options: A. Net promoter score
B. Cost versus benefit analysis
C. Average app availability and uptime
D. Lowest cost with highest potential benefit
explanation: Net promoter score (NPS) is a customer-focused measure that gauges the willingness of customers to recommend a product or service to others, directly measuring overall customer satisfaction and loyalty. While cost-benefit analysis, app availability, and cost-benefit prioritization are useful project management tools, they do not specifically measure customer satisfaction and loyalty from a product owner's perspective.
topic: Product Owner Responsibilities and Agile Metrics

question: You are a project manager in an agile team developing new features for an app. An executive stakeholder with high influence in the organization approaches you and asks you to add a feature that they like. Their friends also think it's a good idea. How should you proceed?
options: A. Place the feature in the product backlog and prioritize it using cost versus benefit
B. Raise a change request to add the new feature into the scope baseline
C. Gather the requirements for the new feature and ask your team to elaborate on it
D. Analyze the impact to your project schedule and cost and add the feature to your project scope
explanation: Before making any scope changes, the feature should be analyzed objectively using a prioritization method like cost versus benefit analysis rather than relying on opinion or confirmation bias. This ensures critical thinking and data-driven decision-making. Raising a change request, gathering requirements, or analyzing impact should only occur after determining whether the feature should actually be pursued.

question: Your project team are gathering solution ideas for a particular customer requirement which is prioritized to be worked on next. The item is complicated and they have been trying to find a way forward for the past 3 weeks. There is not enough work for the upcoming sprint. What should you do?
options: A. Start work on the item in the next sprint anyway and elaborate as the sprint begins
B. Arrange the right people to elaborate the issue with a spike
C. Deprioritize the item until the team can figure out the issue
D. Adjust the project schedule to reflect the extra time taken on elaboration
explanation: A spike is a time-boxed research activity to clarify requirements and figure out a path forward. By arranging the right people and time-boxing it (typically 2 days), you create a deadline that prevents scope creep and ensures the requirement is elaborated before development work begins. This keeps work flowing in agile by having some team members work on the spike while others continue development work.
topic: Agile Elaboration, Spikes, and Time Boxing

question: Your project team have planned the requirements, scope, schedule, and cost for constructing a new building in your organization. An executive from another area approaches you as your project is funded and about to begin execution and asks if you can help them deliver a website as part of your work. What will you do next?
options: A. Raise the request as an issue in your project's issue log
B. Add the website to your project scope and adjust your project scope, schedule, and cost
C. Raise a change request for the added scope and review the impact to your project schedule and cost
D. Offer to help them create a business case for the new idea and get funding for a new project
explanation: Building a website is fundamentally different from constructing a physical building and requires different resources, expertise, and potentially different funding. This represents a separate project that needs its own business case, project charter, and funding rather than being added to an existing construction project. A business case provides a value proposition and financial analysis necessary for formal project approval and kickoff.
topic: Scope Management and Business Case Development

question: You are working on an agile project and have gathered feature ideas from various parts of your organization including frontline staff, executives, and direct customer research. You have many feature ideas to work with. What should you do next?
options: A. Break down each feature into epics and user stories, then size the work with your team to determine effort level
B. Add all items into your product backlog and work on the ones requested by managers first
C. Work with the requesters and your technical experts to create a business model canvas for each feature
D. Perform precedence diagramming on each feature to determine their dependencies
explanation: Working with requesters and technical experts to create a business model canvas for each feature provides a one-page visual summary describing the value proposition, infrastructure, customers, and finances. This gives the information needed to prioritize effectively based on benefit and cost. While breaking down into epics and stories could work, it's premature before prioritization. Adding all items and working on manager-requested items first ignores the need to prioritize by benefit and cost. Precedence diagramming requires more detailed information about the solution and dependencies, which you don't have at this stage.

question: You are working in a new agile team where the work has started to slow and there is uncertainty and low team engagement. You have set time to create a team charter with your team and have agreed on roles, responsibilities, and team cadence. What should you do next?
options: A. Set a team goal for the team to be more engaged in the work over the next 3 months
B. Create a product vision statement showing the value you are bringing and the why behind the work
C. Ask the team to contribute more to all pieces of work during each team meeting
D. Raise it as a people issue for your project and manage it with the project steering committee
explanation: Creating a product vision statement that describes the purpose and why behind the work is a key component of the team charter that will inspire team engagement. This vision statement addresses the root cause of low engagement by helping the team understand the value and purpose of their work.
